------- 2. PaperCoder - 规划 (Planning) -------
[Planning] Overall plan
============================================
Below is a detailed roadmap for reproducing the FOA method (Test‐Time Model Adaptation with Only Forward Passes) along with its experimental evaluation. This document breaks down the overall approach into clear modules—including methodology, implementation details, experimental setup, and evaluation metrics—so that later you can write code with confidence. Note that some details (e.g., the exact form of the activation discrepancy loss in Eqn. (5)) are not fully specified in the paper; in those cases, you should document your design choice and possibly run a few ablations.

──────────────────────────────
1. Environment and Setup

• Language/Framework:
  – Python (e.g., Python 3.8+)
  – PyTorch (for deep learning model implementations)
  – Libraries: timm (to load ViT-Base), torchvision, numpy, pycma (for CMA evolution strategy), and any additional packages (e.g., sklearn or custom implementations for computing ECE).

• Hardware:
  – GPU-based environment during development/testing
  – Note that the method is designed for resource‐constrained devices, so you might later benchmark memory usage.

• Model Quantization:
  – For quantized experiments, use PTQ4ViT (referring to Yuan et al., 2022) to convert the full‐precision ViT-Base model into 8-bit (and optionally 6-bit) versions.

──────────────────────────────
2. Methodology: Key Components and Their Implementation

A. Overall FOA Method
  – The key idea is to adapt a fixed pre-trained model (e.g., ViT-Base) during test time without any backpropagation.
  – Instead of updating the model parameters, a new “prompt” is inserted at the input. This prompt is the only learnable element and is optimized via a derivative‐free optimizer (using CMA-ES).
  – In addition, an activation shifting mechanism is applied at the output feature level to push the final-layer CLS tokens from the OOD distribution back toward the source in‐distribution (ID) statistics.

B. Forward-Only Prompt Adaptation Using CMA-ES

  1. Prompt Insertion:
     – Insert a set of prompt embeddings (learnable tokens) into the network’s input sequence for the Vision Transformer.
     – Hyperparameter: Number of prompt tokens (Nₚ), default set to 3.
     – Initialize the prompt tokens with uniform random values.

  2. Fitness Function:
     – Each candidate prompt is evaluated on a given test batch.
     – The fitness score comprises two components:
         a. Prediction Entropy: Compute the entropy of the model’s output softmax probabilities (e.g., H = –∑ pᶜ log(pᶜ)).
         b. Activation Discrepancy: Compute the error between the batch’s CLS token statistics (mean and standard deviation) and the precomputed source in-distribution statistics {μ_S, σ_S}. One may use an L₂ loss (or another appropriate distance metric) between the batch mean and μ_S (and similarly for the standard deviation). The final loss is a weighted combination:
           
              Fitness L = λ · (Activation Discrepancy) + (1 – λ) · (Prediction Entropy)
           
         – λ is a trade-off parameter; the paper suggests setting it to 0.4 × (BS/64) for ImageNet-C/V2/Sketch and 0.2 × (BS/64) for ImageNet-R.
         – Note: If the exact formula is not fully clear, cite that you are approximating the discrepancy as the L₂ difference, and later run sensitivity tests.

  3. CMA-ES Optimization:
     – Use the pycma library (or a custom implementation) to optimize the inserted prompt.
     – In each iteration (i.e., for each test batch):
         a. Sample K candidate prompts from a parameterized multivariate normal distribution: K = 28 (computed as 4 + 3 × log(prompt_dim), where “prompt_dim” is the flattened dimension of the inserted prompt).
         b. For each candidate:
               • Concatenate or insert the candidate prompt into the input.
               • Forward propagate the test batch through the network.
               • Compute the fitness score using the combined loss function described above.
         c. Rank the candidates by fitness values.
         d. Update the parameters of the search (mean m, covariance matrix Σ, step size τ) according to the CMA-ES update rules.
         e. Finalize the best candidate prompt to be used for the current test batch predictions.
     – Ensure that all optimization is “forward-only” (i.e., do not use PyTorch’s backward pass).

C. Back-to-Source Activation Shifting (Feature-Level Adaptation)
  
  1. For each test batch, after obtaining the final-layer CLS tokens:
     – Compute the batch’s average activation μ_test.
     – Maintain an exponential moving average (EMA) of the overall test activation statistics:
           
              μ_N(t) = α · μ_batch + (1 – α) · μ_N(t–1)
           
           where α is set to 0.1.
     – Define the shifting direction d:
           
              d = μ_S – μ_N(t)
           
         where μ_S is the source in-distribution mean of the CLS token, computed (once) from a small set (e.g., 32 samples) of source data.
     – Shift the CLS token for prediction:
           
              e'_CLS = e_CLS + γ · d
         
         where γ (step size) is set to 1.0.
     – Use the shifted activation for final prediction via the classification head.
  
  2. This mechanism directly aligns test samples with the source distribution without altering model weights.

──────────────────────────────
3. Experimental Setup

A. Datasets
  – Primary benchmarks:
     • ImageNet-C: Use severity level 5 for various corruptions (e.g., Gaussian noise, blur, weather, digital corruptions).
     • ImageNet-R: Artistic renditions of ImageNet classes.
     • ImageNet-V2: A new test set matching the original ImageNet distribution.
     • ImageNet-Sketch: Black and white sketches of ImageNet images.
  – In addition, if desired, simulate non-i.i.d. scenarios:
     • Online imbalanced label distribution shifts.
     • Mixed domain shifts in a continuous test data stream.
  – Preprocessing:
     • Use standard preprocessing pipelines as used for ViT models.
     • For source statistics, collect about 32 unlabeled examples from ImageNet-1K validation or training set.

B. Model and Quantization
  – Use ViT-Base (from timm) as the source model pre-trained on ImageNet-1K.
  – Experiment with both full-precision (32-bit) and quantized (8-bit and optionally 6-bit) versions.
     • For quantization, follow PTQ4ViT instructions.
  
C. Experimental Settings and Hyperparameters
  – Batch size (BS): Default = 64 for most experiments.
  – Population Size (K): 28 (by default).
  – Number of prompt tokens (Nₚ): Default = 3.
  – Trade-off parameter λ: 0.4×(BS/64) for ImageNet-C/V2/Sketch and 0.2×(BS/64) for ImageNet-R.
  – CMA-ES Settings: Use standard settings from the literature (with the above K) and tune if necessary.
  – Activation Shifting: γ = 1.0 and moving average factor α = 0.1.
  – For FOA-I (single sample adaptation variant): implement an interval update strategy where you update the prompt after a fixed interval I (e.g., I = 4) samples.
  
D. Comparison Methods
  – Plan to implement baselines without adaptation (NoAdapt) and compare against:
     • Gradient‐free methods like LAME and T3A.
     • Gradient‐based methods like TENT, SAR, and CoTTA.
  – Ensure that the batch size, prompt dimensions, and other settings remain consistent across comparisons.

E. Evaluation Metrics
  – Classification Accuracy (%): Evaluate over all test samples for each OOD benchmark.
  – Expected Calibration Error (ECE) (%): Compute using standard methods (see Naeini et al., 2015).
  – Optionally report memory usage and runtime metrics, especially when comparing forward-only adaptation to backprop-based methods.
  
F. Ablations and Sensitivity Analyses
  – Sensitivity to number of prompt tokens (try values from 1 to 10).
  – Effect of the number of source samples for calculating {μ_S, σ_S} (test 16, 32, 64, …).
  – Impact of different population sizes K in CMA-ES.
  – Compare results with and without the activation shifting module.
  – Explore different update intervals (I) for FOA-I when using batch size = 1.
  – Monitor and plot the evolution of the fitness function, candidate prompt variations, and CMA-ES internal parameters for debugging and understanding convergence.

──────────────────────────────
4. Implementation Plan / Module Structure

A. Data Loader Module
  – Implement routines to load and preprocess ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-V2, and ImageNet-Sketch datasets.
  – Support extraction of source data (e.g., 32 images) for computing μ_S and σ_S.

B. Model & Prompt Wrapper Module
  – Load pre-trained ViT-Base from timm.
  – Insert an additional prompt module into the network’s input processing pipeline.
  – Build an interface to output both the final classification logits and the CLS token activation from the last transformer block.

C. FOA Algorithm Module
  – Implement the forward-pass adaptation loop:
     • For each test batch:
         ◦ Use CMA-ES to generate K candidate prompt embeddings.
         ◦ For each candidate:
             ▪ Modify the input by concatenating/inserting the candidate prompt.
             ▪ Perform forward pass.
             ▪ Compute fitness using prediction entropy and activation discrepancy.
         ◦ Update CMA-ES distribution parameters (mean, covariance, step size).
         ◦ Select the best candidate prompt.
  – Implement the activation shifting:
     • Compute the batch-level CLS token mean.
     • Use EMA to update the running test feature center.
     • Compute the shift direction (d = μ_S – updated μ_test) and adjust CLS token by γ · d.
     • Propagate final predictions using shifted activations.
     
D. CMA-ES Integration
  – Either wrap around pycma’s API or write a simplified version to sample and update candidate prompts.
  – Pass candidate prompts into the FOA module and receive fitness evaluations back for CMA update.

E. Evaluation & Logging Module
  – Compute evaluation metrics (classification accuracy, ECE).
  – Log per-batch fitness scores, candidate prompt parameters, and adaptation performance.
  – Optionally, save intermediate results and visualizations (e.g., fitness evolution curves, distribution alignment before/after activation shifting).

──────────────────────────────
5. Uncertainties & Design Decisions to Clarify

• Exact Formulation of the Activation Discrepancy Term:
  – The paper mentions aligning the test batch’s CLS activation statistics to the precomputed source statistics. You need to decide whether to use simple L₂ differences, a KL divergence, or another measure for the discrepancy.
  
• Detailed Fitness Function Implementation:
  – The exact weighting and formulation between prediction entropy and activation discrepancy might require empirical tuning. Document the choices and run sensitivity tests.

• Handling of Single-Sample Adaptation (FOA-I):
  – The paper discusses an interval update strategy when BS = 1 (FOA-I). Ensure that your implementation buffers the necessary data until the update interval is reached. Experiment with different intervals (e.g., I = 4, 8, 16, …).

• Quantized Model Adaptation:
  – When adapting quantized models, follow PTQ4ViT guidelines closely (e.g., model weight adjustments, non-differentiable layers) since backpropagation is not used; verify that forward passes run correctly.

──────────────────────────────
6. Reproduction Workflow Summary

1. Environment Setup:
   – Install dependencies (PyTorch, timm, pycma, PTQ4ViT, etc.).
2. Data Preparation:
   – Set up loaders for ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-Sketch.
   – Compute source statistics from a small set of ImageNet-1K samples.
3. Model Initialization:
   – Load the pre-trained ViT-Base.
   – Insert a prompt module and verify forward passes.
4. Implement FOA:
   – Develop the CMA-ES-based prompt optimization loop.
   – Implement computation of prediction entropy and activation discrepancy.
   – Integrate back-to-source activation shifting.
5. Experimental Run:
   – Run experiments on each dataset.
   – Log classification accuracy and ECE.
   – Test variations: full-precision vs. quantized, different batch sizes, and ablations (removing activation shifting, varying prompt count, etc.).
6. Analysis:
   – Compare performance with baseline methods (TENT, T3A, SAR, LAME, etc.).
   – Perform sensitivity analyses and generate plots/tables similar to those in the paper.
7. Documentation:
   – Record all hyperparameters and design choices.
   – Note any deviations or clarifications due to missing details from the paper.

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion

This detailed plan outlines how to implement FOA’s forward-pass-only adaptation using CMA-ES for prompt optimization combined with an activation shifting mechanism. It includes step-by-step guidance—from data preparation and model modifications to CMA-ES integration and evaluation metrics. Clarify any ambiguous details (especially the exact loss formulations) via ablation studies and sensitivity analyses. With this roadmap, you will have a strong foundation to write the final code and reproduce the experimental results reported in the paper.

    "Program call flow": "sequenceDiagram\n    participant M as Main\n    participant DL as DatasetLoader\n    participant MW as ModelWrapper\n    participant CO as CMAOptimizer\n    participant TR as Trainer\n    participant EV as Evaluation\n\n    M->>DL: load_data()\n    DL-->>M: (source_data, test_data, extra_test)\n\n    M->>MW: initialize model with parameters (including prompt insertion)\n    M->>CO: initialize CMAOptimizer with prompt_dim and population size\n    M->>TR: create Trainer(model=MW, optimizer=CO, config)\n\n    loop For each test batch in test_data\n        TR->>CO: sample_candidates()\n        loop For each candidate prompt in candidates\n            TR->>MW: insert candidate prompt into test batch\n            MW->>MW: forward pass obtaining predictions and cls tokens\n            TR->>TR: compute fitness (combining prediction entropy and activation discrepancy with source_stats)\n        end\n        CO->>CO: update distribution using candidate fitness scores\n        CO->>TR: get_best_candidate()\n        TR->>MW: update test batch by inserting best prompt\n        TR->>MW: perform activation shifting using EMA-updated test stats and source_stats\n    end\n    TR-->>M: Training/Adaptation complete\n\n    M->>EV: initialize Evaluation(model=MW, test_loader=test_data, config)\n    EV->>MW: forward pass on test_data\n    MW-->>EV: predictions and activations\n    EV->>EV: compute accuracy and ECE metrics\n    EV-->>M: return evaluation metrics",

## Code: config.yaml
```yaml
## config.yaml
training:
  batch_size: 64                    # Batch size as specified in the paper
  population_size: 28               # CMA-ES population size (4 + 3 * log(prompt_dim))
  prompt_embeddings: 3              # Number of prompt tokens inserted into the input
  prompt_init_method: "uniform"     # Initialization method for the prompt tokens
  lambda_imagenet: 0.4               # Trade-off parameter for ImageNet-C, V2, and Sketch (0.4 * BS/64)
  lambda_imagenet_r: 0.2             # Trade-off parameter for ImageNet-R (0.2 * BS/64)
  activation_shifting_step_size: 1.0  # Step size (gamma) for activation shifting (Eqn. 7)
  ema_factor: 0.1                   # Exponential moving average factor (alpha) for dynamic test statistics (Eqn. 9)
  prompt_sgd_learning_rate: 0.01     # Learning rate for SGD-based prompt update (used in ablation experiments)
  source_sample_num: 32             # Number of source samples to compute in-distribution statistics
  # Note: Adaptation is performed online; 'epochs' are not applicable.
model:
  name: "ViT-Base"                  # Pre-trained Vision Transformer model from timm repository
  precision: "32-bit"               # Model precision; use 32-bit for full precision, quantized models require PTQ4ViT
  quantization: false               # Set to true for quantized experiments (e.g., 8-bit or 6-bit)
datasets:
  primary: "ImageNet-C"
  secondary: ["ImageNet-R", "ImageNet-V2", "ImageNet-Sketch"]
adaptation:
  use_activation_shifting: true     # Enable back-to-source activation shifting mechanism
  update_interval: 1                # Interval for prompt update (set >1 for FOA-I variant with batch size 1)
optimizer:
  type: "CMA-ES"                   # Optimizer for forward-only prompt adaptation
  cma_params:
    sigma0: 0.5                   # Initial standard deviation for CMA-ES (example value)
    max_iter: 10                  # Maximum iterations per test batch for the CMA-ES loop
evaluation:
  metrics: ["accuracy", "ECE"]      # Evaluation metrics: classification accuracy and Expected Calibration Error
logging:
  level: "INFO"
  save_results: true
```

🌟 Usage Summary 🌟

# config.py – Logic Analysis and Configuration for FOA Method

"""
This file centralizes all configuration parameters and hyperparameters used across the FOA (Forward-Only Adaptation) framework.
It is imported by all modules (dataset_loader, model, cma_optimizer, trainer, evaluation, main) to ensure consistency.

Core parameters and their roles:

1. Training Parameters:
   ---------------------
   • batch_size (default 64):
       - Sets the number of test samples per forward pass.
       - Affects the computed prediction entropy and activation discrepancy, and is used to scale the trade‐off lambda.
   • population_size (default 28):
       - Sets the number of candidate solutions (prompt embeddings) to sample per CMA‐ES iteration.
       - Derived from 4 + 3×log(prompt_dim), which in our setting has been preset to 28.
   • prompt_embeddings (default 3):
       - Number of learnable prompt tokens inserted into the ViT input sequence.
   • prompt_init_method ("uniform"):
       - Determines how the prompt tokens are initialized. Here “uniform” implies a uniform random initialization.
   • lambda_imagenet (default 0.4):
       - Trade‐off parameter for datasets such as ImageNet-C, ImageNet-V2, and ImageNet-Sketch.
       - In practice, its effective value is scaled as (0.4 * BS/64).
   • lambda_imagenet_r (default 0.2):
       - Trade‐off parameter specifically for ImageNet-R (artistic renditions), scaled as (0.2 * BS/64).
       - These lambdas balance the two components of the fitness function: prediction entropy and activation discrepancy.
   • activation_shifting_step_size (gamma, default 1.0):
       - Step size used during the back-to-source activation shifting of the CLS tokens.
       - Implements the shifting e'_CLS = e_CLS + γ * d, where d is the difference between source and test CLS token means.
   • ema_factor (alpha, default 0.1):
       - Factor used for the exponential moving average (EMA) to update the running test statistics.
       - This is critical to smooth the noisy batch statistics and stabilize the activation shifting.
   • prompt_sgd_learning_rate (default 0.01):
       - Although our primary optimizer for prompt adaptation is CMA-ES, this rate is specified for possible SGD-based ablation experiments.
   • source_sample_num (default 32):
       - The number of source (in-distribution) samples from ImageNet-1K used to compute the source CLS token statistics (mean and std).
       - These statistics form the reference distribution for computing the activation discrepancy.

2. Model Parameters:
   --------------------
   • name ("ViT-Base"):
       - Specifies the pre-trained Vision Transformer model (from timm) used for adaptation.
   • precision ("32-bit"):
       - Indicates full precision of the model. For quantized experiments, this parameter will be changed and PTQ4ViT tools applied.
   • quantization (false):
       - A flag indicating whether to run quantized experiments (e.g., 8-bit or 6-bit). When true, quantization methods such as PTQ4ViT will be invoked.

3. Dataset Parameters:
   ----------------------
   • primary ("ImageNet-C"):
       - Primary evaluation dataset used for prompt adaptation and performance measurement (with severity level 5).
   • secondary (["ImageNet-R", "ImageNet-V2", "ImageNet-Sketch"]):
       - List of additional test datasets enabling OOD generalization experiments.

4. Adaptation Parameters:
   -------------------------
   • use_activation_shifting (true):
       - Enables the back-to-source activation shifting mechanism.
       - When enabled, after the forward pass the CLS tokens are shifted toward the source in-distribution mean.
   • update_interval (default 1):
       - Determines how often the prompt is updated. A value >1 is used for FOA-I (single sample adaptation variant) where an interval strategy is needed.

5. Optimizer Parameters:
   -----------------------
   • type ("CMA-ES"):
       - Specifies that the derivative-free Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy is used to optimize the prompt.
   • cma_params:
       - Contains parameters to configure CMA-ES:
           ▪ sigma0 (default 0.5): Initial standard deviation (step size) for the sampling distribution.
           ▪ max_iter (default 10): The maximum number of CMA-ES iterations per test batch.
       - These settings guide the prompt optimization process over the high-dimensional prompt space.

6. Evaluation Parameters:
   -------------------------
   • metrics (["accuracy", "ECE"]):
       - Lists evaluation metrics: classification accuracy (percentage) and Expected Calibration Error (ECE, percentage).
       - ECE is computed using standard methods (e.g., following Naeini et al., 2015).

7. Logging Parameters:
   ---------------------
   • level ("INFO"):
       - Sets the logging verbosity.
   • save_results (true):
       - Indicates whether to persist evaluation and adaptation results to disk.

Usage Notes and Rationale:
---------------------------
- The trade-off parameters (lambda_imagenet and lambda_imagenet_r) directly affect the fitness function,
  which is a weighted sum of the prediction entropy (to encourage confident predictions)
  and the activation discrepancy (to align test CLS token statistics with source distributions).
  Clarify or adjust these values via sensitivity analysis if the balance between the two terms is not optimal.

- The activation shifting mechanism is critical for aligning out-of-distribution test features with the source distribution.
  The EMA factor smooths batch variations, especially when the batch size is small.

- Using a dedicated configuration file ensures that all modules (data loading, model wrapping, CMA-ES optimizer, trainer, evaluation)
  operate with consistent settings. In this way, experimental reproducibility is enhanced.

- Although the precise mathematical formulation for the activation discrepancy is not fully specified
  in the paper, our implementation will likely use a simple L2 loss between the batch mean (and variance, if needed) of the CLS tokens
  and the corresponding source statistics. This configuration parameter will be referenced wherever the loss is computed.

- All parameters shown here should be treated as "the ground truth" for production runs; any further modifications should be logged and justified.

This config.py file will be imported at program startup (e.g., in main.py) to initialize all modules and ensure a reproducible experimental setup.
"""

# (The actual code in config.py will load these values from config.yaml or define them as constants/dictionary structures.)

[ANALYSIS] dataset_loader.py
============================================
Below is a detailed logic analysis for writing the dataset_loader.py module.

────────────────────────────────────────────
1. Purpose and Role in the Overall System

• The DatasetLoader class is responsible for loading, preprocessing, and packaging the various datasets needed for test‐time adaptation experiments. This includes:
  – The primary test dataset (ImageNet-C), which is the main dataset used for evaluation.
  – Additional test-domain datasets (ImageNet-R, ImageNet-V2, and ImageNet-Sketch) for extended evaluations.
  – A small source dataset (e.g., 32 images) drawn from ImageNet (or a similar source set) that is used solely to compute in‐distribution statistics. These statistics (mean and standard deviation of the final CLS tokens) are later used for both the activation discrepancy computation in the fitness function and for activation shifting.

• The module leverages PyTorch’s DataLoader and torchvision transforms so that input images are preprocessed consistently, matching the required pipeline for a ViT model.
  
────────────────────────────────────────────
2. Initialization and Configuration

• In the __init__ method of the DatasetLoader class, the following steps are planned:
  – Accept a configuration dictionary (from config.py) and extract key parameters such as:
      • The overall batch size (e.g., 64 from training.batch_size).
      • The number of source images to load (source_sample_num set to 32).
      • Any dataset-specific paths or identifiers (even if not explicitly given in config.yaml, one can assume default folder paths or configurable defaults).
  – Set up image preprocessing pipelines using torchvision.transforms. A typical transform may include:
      • Resize (e.g., resizing the shorter side to a fixed size like 256).
      • Center crop to the target resolution (e.g., 224×224 for ViT-Base).
      • Conversion to tensor.
      • Normalization with ImageNet mean and standard deviation.  
    This transformation chain ensures that inputs are in the exact format expected by timm’s implementation of ViT-Base.

• It may also be useful to specify separate transforms for training (source sample collection) and testing if any subtle differences are needed. However, for FOA the emphasis is on test-time adaptation where no augmentation is expected.

────────────────────────────────────────────
3. Loading the Datasets

• The key method load_data() will perform the following:

  A. Source Dataset Loader:
    – Load the source images (for example from a directory that contains a subset of the ImageNet training or validation set).
    – Apply the same transform as the test images because later the CLS tokens computed from these images are used as the source in‐distribution statistics.
    – Use torch.utils.data.Subset (or a custom selection logic) to limit the dataset size to the source_sample_num (32 images). This small dataset is assumed to be unlabeled; labels are not needed because it is used only for calculating the mean and standard deviation of the CLS token activations.
    – Wrap this dataset in a DataLoader (batch size could be equal to the total number of source samples to get the full statistics in one forward pass).

  B. Primary Test Dataset Loader (ImageNet-C):
    – Load ImageNet-C images from its assumed folder structure. If the dataset is stored using ImageFolder organizing images by corruption types, the loader should be directed to the correct root directory.
    – Apply the test-time transform pipeline.
    – Use the batch size specified in the config (64 by default).
  
  C. Additional Test Domains Loader:
    – For each dataset name listed in the secondary field of the config (ImageNet-R, ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-Sketch), construct a separate dataset loader.
    – Again, use torchvision.datasets.ImageFolder (or a custom dataset class if needed) with the same transform pipeline.
    – Return either a list or a mapping (e.g., a dictionary keyed by dataset name) of DataLoader instances.

• In cases where the file/directory layout of each dataset differs, the DatasetLoader module should abstract these details – for example, by defining default paths or expecting paths from command-line arguments or additional config parameters.

────────────────────────────────────────────
4. Data Structures and Return Interfaces

• Following the design’s interface, the load_data() routine should return three objects:
  – Source DataLoader: used to compute in-distribution statistics (CLS token mean and std).
  – Primary Test DataLoader: corresponding to the main evaluation dataset (ImageNet-C).
  – Additional Test Domains DataLoader(s): for the other datasets (ImageNet-R, ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-Sketch).

• The return format might be a tuple such as (source_loader, test_loader, additional_loaders) where additional_loaders can be a list or dictionary.

────────────────────────────────────────────
5. Special Considerations

• Preprocessing Consistency:
  – It is important that the same transform is applied to both the source and test images because the downstream adaptation process compares the CLS token activations computed from the source images (to form μ_S and σ_S) with those computed from the test batches. Ensuring that both datasets undergo exactly the same image normalization and resizing is critical.
  
• Folder Structure Awareness:
  – Although not fully specified, the implementation should either assume a standard directory structure or be made flexible to receive dataset paths through extra config parameters or command-line arguments. This will ease the reproduction of the experiments as described in the paper.

• Handling of Labels:
  – While labels might be present in the dataset (for evaluation of classification accuracy), they are not used in computing the source statistics or for the adaptation process. However, during evaluation, the Evaluation module uses these labels for computing metrics like classification accuracy and ECE. Therefore, labels should be loaded correctly and passed forward but can be ignored by the adaptation logic.

• Debugging and Logging:
  – The module should include logging (as configured by logging level in config.yaml) to indicate successful data loading, the number of samples loaded in each dataset, and any potential issues with file paths.
  
────────────────────────────────────────────
6. Pseudocode Outline

The DatasetLoader class may be outlined as follows:

-----------------------------------------------------
Class DatasetLoader:
    • __init__(self, config: dict):
         - Save config parameters (batch_size, source_sample_num, dataset names, possibly dataset directory paths).
         - Define default torchvision.transform pipelines (Resize, CenterCrop, ToTensor, Normalize).

    • load_data(self) -> (source_loader, test_loader, additional_loaders):
         - For the source dataset:
             • Use torchvision.datasets.ImageFolder with source directory.
             • Apply the defined transforms.
             • Optionally, use torch.utils.data.Subset to select self.source_sample_num images.
             • Wrap in torch.utils.data.DataLoader (batch_size = self.source_sample_num or a small fixed value).
         - For the primary test dataset (ImageNet-C):
             • Use torchvision.datasets.ImageFolder with test directory for ImageNet-C.
             • Apply the same transforms.
             • Create a DataLoader with the training batch_size.
         - For additional test datasets:
             • Loop over the names in config.datasets.secondary:
                 – Determine the directory path (either preset defaults or parameterized).
                 – Use ImageFolder + transforms.
                 – Create DataLoader with batch_size, then store in a dictionary keyed by dataset name.
         - Return (source_loader, test_loader, additional_loaders).

-----------------------------------------------------
  
────────────────────────────────────────────
7. Summary

This module encapsulates all data loading logic ensuring that:
  – All images (source as well as test) are preprocessed using the same transformations.
  – Only a small subset of source images (32 samples as defined in config) is loaded to compute the reference CLS token statistics.
  – Different datasets (both primary and secondary) are returned as separate DataLoader instances so that later modules (such as evaluation and adaptation in Trainer) can iterate over them reliably.
  
By following this detailed logic, the dataset_loader.py module will robustly support the reproduction of the FOA method’s experiments across multiple test domains with correct and consistent preprocessing required for Vision Transformer models.
  
────────────────────────────────────────────
End of dataset_loader.py Logic Analysis. 29%|██▊       | 2/7 [00:51<02:10, 26.09s/it] 43%|████▎     | 3/7 [01:23<01:53, 28.47s/it]

[ANALYSIS] model.py
============================================
Below is our detailed logical analysis for the ModelWrapper class (model.py). This module’s role is to wrap a pre‐trained ViT-Base model (loaded from timm) and augment it with additional functionality required for FOA. In particular, the ModelWrapper must:

1. Load the Base Model and Initialize the Prompt Tokens  
 • In the __init__ method, the class loads the pre‐trained ViT-Base model (using timm) from the configuration (e.g. "name": "ViT-Base").  
 • Retrieve the model’s embedding dimension (typically d ≈ 768 for ViT-Base) so that the dimensions of the prompt tokens can be matched.  
 • Based on the config parameter "prompt_embeddings" (default 3) and the chosen "prompt_init_method" (e.g., “uniform”), create a learnable prompt tensor of shape (1, Nₚ, d).  
  – Although FOA adapts the prompt via a derivative‐free CMA-ES routine, we still need a “placeholder” (or initial value) stored inside the ModelWrapper.  
  – (Optional) If working in full precision versus quantized (as indicated by the config), additional branches may be added later; however, the main logic stays similar.

2. Inserting Prompt Tokens into the Input Sequence  
 • Define an insert_prompts(x, prompt) method:
  – Expecting x to be in the “tokenized” space (i.e. the ViT’s patch embeddings plus class token).  
  – The typical ViT input sequence starts with the class token (CLS) followed by patch tokens.
  – In insertion, we create a new token sequence where the prompt tokens are concatenated immediately after the CLS token. For example:  
   augmented_tokens = concat( x[:, 0:1, :], prompt.repeat(B,1,1), x[:, 1:, :] )  
  – This new sequence is then fed into the transformer layers.
 • Note: The caller (likely the Trainer) may supply a candidate prompt tensor (sampled via CMA) to this method. The inserted prompt must have the same embedding dimensions as the model’s other tokens.

3. Forward Pass Method  
 • Implement a forward(x, prompt=None) method:
  – If a candidate prompt is provided as argument, call insert_prompts(x, prompt) to produce the augmented token sequence.
  – Otherwise, either use the default stored prompt or simply run the base model forward.
  – Once the tokens (with prompts inserted) are available, run them through the transformer layers and obtain the final output logits via the model’s classification head.
  – Return the logits (and, if needed, the intermediate activations).
 • This method must be “forward‐only” (i.e. no backpropagation is done as per the FOA paradigm).

4. Extracting CLS Token Activations  
 • Define a get_cls_tokens(x, prompt=None) method:
  – Similar to the forward() method, run the input x (with an optional prompt inserted) through the model until the last transformer block.
  – Then extract the CLS token’s embedding (typically the first token of the output sequence, i.e. tokens[:, 0, :]).
  – Return the CLS token feature for further evaluation (e.g., computing activation discrepancy).
 • This method is used by the Trainer during fitness computation (to compare against the precomputed source statistics).

5. Activation Shifting on the CLS Tokens  
 • Define an activation_shift(cls_token, source_mean, test_mean, gamma) method:
  – This method implements the back-to-source activation shifting described in the paper.
  – The test_mean is expected to have been computed externally using an exponential moving average (EMA) over test batches (with the EMA factor provided in the config, e.g., ema_factor: 0.1).
  – Compute the shifting direction d as:  
   d = source_mean – test_mean  
  – Then, shift the given CLS token:  
   shifted_cls = cls_token + gamma * d  
  – Here, gamma corresponds to the "activation_shifting_step_size" from config (default 1.0).
  – Return the shifted CLS token which (after being fed into the classification head) will yield final predictions.
 • This method encapsulates the idea of “moving” the OOD activation back toward the ID center (source_mean).

Additional Considerations:  
 • All methods must respect the model’s architecture and preserve tensor dimensions.  
 • The insert_prompts and forward methods need to handle batch dimensions and repeat the prompt for every sample in the batch.  
 • In our implementation, the conversion from raw images to patch embeddings is done by the underlying ViT model. We are intervening only in the token sequence (by inserting prompt tokens) and later by modifying the CLS token through activation shifting.  
 • The overall design anticipates that the Trainer module will call these methods to build the candidate pipelines (e.g., sample prompts via CMA-ES, perform forward passes, compute fitness metrics, and then choose the best prompt for prediction).  
 • The ModelWrapper should work seamlessly with both full-precision and quantized versions of the model (with the latter possibly having specific nuances in how forward passes are computed, although these details are handled upstream during quantization).

To summarize, ModelWrapper in model.py must:

• Load a pre-trained ViT-Base from timm  
• Hold an initialized learnable prompt (using settings from config.yaml)  
• Provide an insert_prompts() method to augment the input tokens by concatenating the prompt tokens right after the CLS token  
• Provide a forward() method that—given (optionally) a candidate prompt—processes the input through the entire model to produce output logits  
• Provide a get_cls_tokens() helper that extracts the final-layer CLS token for computing adaptation losses  
• Provide an activation_shift() method that implements the back-to-source shifting of the CLS token using the EMA-updated test statistics and the precomputed source statistics

This analysis will guide the code in model.py, ensuring that our ModelWrapper meets the design interfaces and integrates seamlessly with other modules (e.g., Trainer and CMAOptimizer) for the FOA method.
============================================

[ANALYSIS] cma_optimizer.py
============================================
Below is the detailed logic analysis for the CMAOptimizer module in cma_optimizer.py:

────────────────────────────────────────────
Overview:
• The CMAOptimizer class is responsible for handling the derivative‐free optimization of the prompt embeddings using CMA-ES (Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy). It wraps the pycma library so that the Trainer module can sample candidate prompts, update the underlying distribution based on their fitness scores, and retrieve the best candidate prompt after each adaptation iteration. All operations are performed without using any backpropagation.

────────────────────────────────────────────
Class Initialization (__init__ method):
• Inputs:
  - prompt_dim: An integer representing the flattened dimension of the prompt embeddings. (For instance, if there are Nₚ prompt tokens and each token has a dimension d, then prompt_dim = Nₚ × d.)
  - population_size: The number of candidate prompts to sample per iteration (read from config as 28).
  - cma_params: A dictionary holding parameters for the CMA‑ES algorithm. At minimum, this will include:
      · sigma0 (initial standard deviation, e.g., 0.5 as given in config)
      · max_iter (maximum iterations allowed per test batch adaptation loop)
      · (Other optional parameters such as restart criteria or logging options can be provided.)
• Initialization Steps:
  - Determine the initial mean vector. According to the paper and our design notes, the prompt embedding’s mean can be set to the zero vector (m(0)=0) or be sampled using the "uniform" method (we could sample uniformly from a small range if desired). For simplicity, set initial_mean = numpy.zeros(prompt_dim) or use a uniform random sample based on the configuration.
  - Prepare an options dictionary for the CMA-ES algorithm incorporating:
      · popsize = population_size.
      · sigma0 (from cma_params).
      · maxiter (from cma_params), if relevant.
  - Create an instance of the CMAEvolutionStrategy from the pycma library using the call:
         es = cma.CMAEvolutionStrategy(initial_mean, sigma0, options)
  - Save this instance as an attribute (e.g., self.es) for use in later methods.
  - Store any other practical internal variables if needed (for example, to cache the current best candidate, iteration count, etc.).
  
────────────────────────────────────────────
Method: sample_candidates
• Responsibility:
  - Generate a set of candidate prompt embeddings from the current multivariate normal distribution defined by the current mean, covariance matrix, and step size maintained by self.es.
• Implementation Steps:
  - Call the pycma method self.es.ask() to retrieve a list of candidate solutions. This returns a list (or array) of numpy arrays, each of dimension prompt_dim.
  - Convert each candidate from a numpy array to a torch.Tensor (using torch.from_numpy) and, if necessary, ensure it is in the proper floating-point format (e.g., torch.float32).
  - Optionally, you might reshape the candidate if the ModelWrapper expects a tensor with shape (Nₚ, d) instead of a flat vector.
  - Return the list (or a stacked tensor) of candidate prompt embeddings. This list will later be used in the forward pass of the model (via the Trainer) to compute fitness scores.

────────────────────────────────────────────
Method: update
• Responsibility:
  - Update the CMA-ES distribution parameters (mean, covariance matrix, step size) using the fitness scores computed for the candidate prompts.
• Inputs:
  - candidates: The same set of candidate prompt embeddings that were produced by sample_candidates. These are provided as torch.Tensors.
  - fitness_scores: A list (or array) of fitness values (floats) corresponding to each candidate. Note: Lower fitness is interpreted as “better” if we assume the fitness function is formulated as a loss.
• Implementation Steps:
  - Convert each candidate (if not already done) from torch.Tensor back to a numpy array.
  - Call the pycma update method (typically using self.es.tell(solutions, fitness_scores)) where "solutions" are the candidate arrays and "fitness_scores" are the corresponding evaluations.
  - This call updates the internal mean, covariance matrix, and step size based on the ranking of fitness values.
  - Optionally, log or display the current best fitness value (using self.es.disp() or similar debugging messages) for troubleshooting.
  - No explicit return value is needed – the internal state of self.es is updated in place.

────────────────────────────────────────────
Method: get_best_candidate
• Responsibility:
  - Retrieve and return the candidate prompt that has, according to CMA-ES, the best (lowest) fitness value.
• Implementation Steps:
  - Use pycma’s built-in method to get the best candidate. For example, self.es.result.xbest (or self.es.best.x) contains the best candidate found so far.
  - Convert the best candidate (a numpy array) to a torch.Tensor.
  - Reshape it if necessary (e.g., from a flat vector to a tensor with shape [prompt_embeddings, token_dim]) so that it can be directly inserted into the ModelWrapper’s input.
  - Return the best candidate as a torch.Tensor.
  
────────────────────────────────────────────
Additional Considerations:
• Data Type Conversions:
  - The integration between PyTorch Tensors and numpy arrays is essential. Use conversion functions (torch.from_numpy and numpy.asarray) carefully to ensure numerical precision is maintained.
• Fitness Interpretation:
  - Clarify whether the fitness scores provided by the Trainer are formulated such that a lower value is better (typical for loss minimization) or whether any inversion is required. In our design, assume that lower fitness indicates a candidate with better performance.
• Iterative Updates:
  - In many test batches, the Trainer may run multiple iterations (up to max_iter) of CMA-ES within a single batch adaptation cycle. The CMAOptimizer class design should allow consecutive calls to sample_candidates → update → sample again if needed.
• Logging and Debugging:
  - It might be useful to include optional logging (e.g., using Python’s logging module or printing via tqdm) so that the evolution of the mean, covariance matrix, or best candidate fitness can be tracked for analysis and debugging.
• Reusability:
  - The design should allow the same instance of CMAOptimizer to be reused for consecutive batches if desired. Alternatively, the Trainer may instantiate a new optimizer per batch if required by the overall adaptation framework.
• Handling High Dimensions:
  - Although prompt_dim is significantly lower than full model parameter dimensions, ensure that pycma is instantiated with appropriate options for the given dimension to avoid convergence issues.

────────────────────────────────────────────
Summary:
The CMAOptimizer module encapsulates the entire workflow of using CMA-ES for prompt optimization:
1. __init__ sets up the CMA-ES search with an initial mean (using either zeros or a uniform initialization), sigma0, population size, and any other specified parameters.
2. sample_candidates retrieves candidate prompt embeddings from the algorithm’s internal distribution.
3. update uses the candidates’ computed fitness values (provided by the Trainer) to update the internal distribution parameters.
4. get_best_candidate retrieves the best prompt (with minimal fitness/loss) to be used for the final adaptation step.

This design fully adheres to the FOA methodology as described in the paper and aligns with the provided configuration values from config.yaml. 57%|█████▋    | 4/7 [01:52<01:25, 28.62s/it]

[ANALYSIS] trainer.py
============================================
Below is a detailed logic analysis for the Trainer module. This module’s primary responsibility is to run the forward‐only adaptation loop over test batches. It leverages the ModelWrapper (which embeds the pre-trained network and prompt injection mechanism) and the CMAOptimizer (which samples and updates candidate prompt embeddings) to adjust the model at test time without any backward pass. The key steps, decisions, and data flows are described below.

──────────────────────────────
1. Trainer Class Responsibilities

• Initialization (__init__):
  - Accept the following inputs:
    ▪ A ModelWrapper instance (which provides methods for forward passing data, inserting prompts, and performing activation shifting).
    ▪ A CMAOptimizer instance (which will handle candidate prompt sampling and distribution updates).
    ▪ A configuration dictionary (loaded from config.yaml) that provides hyperparameters such as batch size, number of prompt tokens, trade-off lambda value, activation shifting step size (gamma), EMA factor, and update interval.
  - Optionally receive or later be provided with source statistics (e.g., source_id_mean and source_id_std computed from a small set of source images) so that activation shifting and activation discrepancy loss can be computed.
  - Initialize additional state variables (e.g., an attribute to hold the running EMA of test CLS token statistics, which is initially None).

──────────────────────────────
2. Key Functions / Methods in Trainer

A. compute_fitness(predictions, cls_activations, source_stats)
   - This function computes a single “fitness” value for a given candidate prompt’s forward pass.
   - Steps:
     1. Prediction Entropy Computation:
        - Convert model output logits (or directly the candidate’s output if already probabilities) to softmax probabilities.
        - Compute entropy for each sample using the formula:  
              entropy = –sum(p * log(p))  
          (Ensure you average over the batch; using torch.no_grad to keep it evaluation-only.)
     2. Activation Discrepancy:
        - Obtain the CLS token activations from the candidate forward pass.
        - Calculate batch-level statistics—for example, the mean (and optionally the standard deviation) across the batch.
        - Compute the discrepancy between this batch mean and the precomputed source in-distribution mean (source_stats["mean"]). (In our design, we choose a simple L₂ distance [e.g., the squared norm of the difference]. Optionally, one could also incorporate the difference in standard deviations.)
     3. Combine the two Components:
        - Use a weighted (convex) combination:
              fitness = λ · (activation discrepancy) + (1 – λ) · (prediction entropy)
        - The trade-off parameter λ is retrieved from the configuration (e.g., config.training.lambda_imagenet for primary datasets or lambda_imagenet_r for ImageNet-R).
        - Since both components are losses that should be minimized, the candidate with the lowest fitness is considered the best.
   - Note: Document your design choice for the activation discrepancy (here a simple L₂ loss is used) so that later ablations can be performed if needed.

B. adapt_prompt(test_batch, source_stats)
   - This method implements the core adaptation loop for a test batch.
   - High-Level Steps:
     1. Sampling Candidate Prompts:
         - Ask the CMAOptimizer to sample a set of candidate prompt embeddings (list/array of tensors). The number of candidates is defined by the population size (e.g., 28) in the configuration.
     2. Evaluate Each Candidate:
         - For each candidate prompt:
           ▪ Use the ModelWrapper’s method (e.g., insert_prompts) to combine the candidate prompt with the test batch images.
           ▪ Perform a forward pass (wrapped in torch.no_grad since no gradients are required) to obtain both:
                • The prediction logits (for computing prediction entropy).
                • The CLS token activations (to later compute activation discrepancy).
           ▪ Call compute_fitness(predictions, cls_activations, source_stats) to obtain the candidate’s fitness score.  
         - Collect the fitness score for all candidates.
     3. Updating the Optimizer:
         - Call the CMAOptimizer’s update method to adjust its internal multivariate normal distribution (mean, covariance, and step size) based on the candidate fitness scores.
         - Retrieve the best candidate’s prompt using get_best_candidate().
     4. Final Forward Pass with Best Prompt:
         - Insert the best prompt into the test batch (again via ModelWrapper) and perform a forward pass to get the “final” predictions and associated CLS tokens.
     5. Activation Shifting:
         - Compute the mean of the CLS token activations for the current test batch.
         - If this is the first batch (or if no running mean is stored), initialize the running test mean (running_test_mean) with the current batch’s mean.
         - Otherwise, update the running mean using the exponential moving average (EMA):
                running_test_mean = α * current_batch_mean + (1 – α) * running_test_mean  
           where α is the EMA factor from the configuration.
         - Compute the shifting direction:
                d = source_mean – running_test_mean
           where source_mean is taken from source_stats.
         - Call the ModelWrapper’s activation_shift method with the current CLS tokens, source_mean, running_test_mean, and step size (gamma from config) to adjust the activations.
         - The shifted activations are then used for the final prediction via the model’s classification head.
     6. Return the final, activation-shifted predictions (and optionally the best candidate prompt).
   - Special Case – FOA-I for Batch Size 1:
     - If the test batch size equals one and the configuration’s update_interval is greater than one, the Trainer should buffer incoming samples.
     - Only when the accumulated buffer reaches the update interval, perform the above adaptation on the concatenated batch.
     - After adaptation, clear the buffer and apply the best prompt to all buffered samples.

C. train(test_loader, source_stats)
   - This method loops over the test data loader and applies the adapt_prompt method to each batch (or aggregated batch in the FOA-I case).
   - It logs the progress (using tqdm and/or the logging module) and may accumulate predictions for later evaluation.
   - The method ensures that for every test batch the forward-only adaptation process is applied, and that internal statistics (such as the running EMA for test features) are updated.

──────────────────────────────
3. Data Flow and Hyperparameter Usage

• The Trainer receives batches from the DatasetLoader (which were preprocessed and loaded with a set batch size – typically 64, as per config).
• Source statistics (μ_S and σ_S for the CLS tokens) are precomputed from a small set of source images (32 by default, as specified in config.training.source_sample_num) and passed into adapt_prompt.
• Throughout the process, several hyperparameters are used:
   - Batch size (from config.training.batch_size)
   - Population size (from config.training.population_size)
   - Number of prompt tokens (config.training.prompt_embeddings)
   - Trade-off parameter λ (from config.training.lambda_imagenet for primary datasets, or lambda_imagenet_r if using ImageNet-R)
   - Activation shifting step size (γ, from config.training.activation_shifting_step_size)
   - EMA factor (α, from config.training.ema_factor)
   - Update interval for FOA-I (config.adaptation.update_interval)
• All forward computations are wrapped within a torch.no_grad context since no gradient calculation is needed.

──────────────────────────────
4. Logging and Debugging

• For each test batch, the Trainer should log:
   - The fitness scores for each candidate prompt.
   - The selected “best” candidate’s fitness.
   - The computed batch-level statistics (mean of CLS tokens) and the updated EMA value.
   - Optionally, intermediate values such as prediction entropy and activation discrepancy components.
• Utilizing tqdm to show progress across batches will aid in debugging and performance tracking.

──────────────────────────────
5. Summary of Flow in trainer.py

When the train() method is called:
  1. For every test batch from test_loader:
     - (If using FOA-I, check the update_interval and accumulate when necessary.)
     - Sample candidate prompts using CMAOptimizer.
     - For each candidate:
         • Insert prompt into the test batch.
         • Forward pass via ModelWrapper → obtain predictions, CLS activations.
         • Compute candidate fitness (combining prediction entropy and activation discrepancy using source_stats).
     - Update the CMA-ES distribution using these fitness scores.
     - Retrieve the best candidate prompt.
     - Perform a final forward pass on the test batch using the best prompt.
     - Compute the batch’s CLS token mean.
     - Update the running EMA test mean.
     - Compute the activation shifting vector d and shift the CLS tokens.
     - Use the shifted activations to produce final predictions.
  2. Log results and (if needed) collect predictions for later evaluation.
  3. End of training/adaptation loop.

──────────────────────────────
6. Final Remarks

• The design adheres strictly to the FOA method: no model weights are updated, only the newly inserted prompt is optimized via a derivative‐free search (CMA-ES), and an activation shifting is applied to the model’s final-layer CLS tokens.
• The choice of measuring activation discrepancy via a simple L₂ loss between current test batch statistics and source statistics is a design decision (it could later be extended or ablated).
• All hyperparameters and constants are taken directly from the configuration file (config.yaml) to prevent any assumptions.
• Finally, care should be taken to isolate all computations in a forward-only (no backprop) manner.

This logic analysis should provide a clear, step-by-step guide for the implementation of trainer.py in the FOA reproduction framework. 71%|███████▏  | 5/7 [02:35<01:08, 34.13s/it]

[ANALYSIS] evaluation.py
============================================
Below is a detailed logic analysis for the Evaluation module (evaluation.py). This module defines an Evaluation class that is responsible for performing forward passes over the test dataset using the adapted (FOA) model and then computing the key evaluation metrics—classification accuracy and Expected Calibration Error (ECE). The analysis below outlines the intended design and step‐by‐step logic.

──────────────────────────────
Overview

• Purpose:
  – The Evaluation class is designed to assess the performance of the adapted model on one or more test datasets.
  – It computes (a) classification accuracy by checking how many predictions (obtained via a forward pass) match the ground truth labels, and (b) ECE which measures the calibration of the predicted probabilities.
  – Both metrics are exactly what the paper uses to evaluate the FOA method.

• Inputs:
  – An adapted model (wrapped in the ModelWrapper, which already integrates prompt insertion and any activation shifting).
  – A PyTorch DataLoader (test_loader) providing batches of test data (both inputs and ground truth labels).
  – A configuration dictionary (loaded from config.yaml) that specifies parameters (e.g., batch size, evaluation metrics, logging details).

──────────────────────────────
Evaluation Class Structure and Methods

1. __init__(self, model, test_loader, config)
   – Stores the model, test_loader, and configuration.
   – This method sets up any required parameters from the configuration (e.g., which metrics to compute) and may also set the logging level.

2. evaluate(self) -> dict
   – This is the main method that orchestrates the evaluation process.
   – It first sets the model into evaluation mode (model.eval()) and wraps the forward pass loop within a torch.no_grad() block to prevent gradient computations (this also saves memory).
   – The method loops over each batch provided by the test_loader. For every batch:
       • It extracts the input data and the corresponding ground truth labels.
       • It performs a forward pass using the model. The ModelWrapper’s forward(x) method is expected to return the class logits.
       • These logits are converted into probabilities using a softmax operation.
       • The predictions (either the raw logits or the probabilities) and the ground truth labels are stored in lists or tensors for eventual aggregation.
   – After processing the entire test set, the method calls:
       • compute_accuracy(predictions, targets) to calculate overall classification accuracy.
       • compute_ece(probabilities, targets, n_bins) to compute the ECE. Here, a standard binning procedure (using, for example, 10–15 bins) will be implemented.
   – Finally, it bundles these metrics into a dictionary (e.g., {"accuracy": acc, "ECE": ece}) and returns that dictionary.

3. compute_accuracy(self, predictions: Tensor, targets: Tensor) -> float
   – This utility function takes a tensor of predictions and target labels.
   – Typically, the predicted class for each instance is determined using argmax (across the output dimension).
   – It then computes the proportion of predictions that match the ground truth.
   – The computed accuracy (as a percentage or fraction) is returned.

4. compute_ece(self, probabilities: np.ndarray, targets: np.ndarray, n_bins: int = 15) -> float
   – This method calculates the Expected Calibration Error.
   – Logic for ECE:
       • First, for each sample, determine the confidence (i.e., the maximum probability) and the predicted label.
       • Partition the range [0, 1] into n_bins (for example, 15 bins).
       • For each confidence bin:
            – Identify samples with confidence values falling into that bin.
            – Calculate the average confidence and the accuracy (i.e., how many predictions in that bin are correct) for samples in the bin.
            – Compute the absolute difference between the average confidence and accuracy for the bin.
            – Weight this difference by the number of samples in the bin relative to the total number of samples.
       • Sum these weighted differences over all bins to obtain the final ECE.
   – The function returns the computed ECE value.
   – Note: Although scikit-learn does not provide a built-in ECE function, the implementation may use its utilities (or rely purely on numpy) to perform the binning and averaging.

──────────────────────────────
Step-by-Step Flow in evaluate()

1. Set Up Evaluation:
   • Call model.eval() and wrap the processing loop in a with torch.no_grad() block.
   • Initialize containers (e.g., lists) to hold batch-level predictions and corresponding targets.

2. Per-Batch Processing:
   • For each (inputs, targets) tuple from test_loader:
       – Pass inputs through the adapted model’s forward() method.
       – Compute softmax over the logits to obtain predicted probabilities.
       – Derive predicted classes using argmax of the probabilities.
       – Append the computed probabilities and targets (and optionally predicted labels) to the container lists.

3. Aggregate Results:
   • After processing all batches, concatenate the stored predictions and targets (converting to numpy arrays if necessary for the ECE computation).
   • Call compute_accuracy to compare the predicted classes with the aggregated targets.
   • Call compute_ece with the aggregated probabilities and targets. The number of bins (n_bins) can be hard-coded or provided via the config if desired.

4. Return Metrics:
   • Combine the computed accuracy and ECE in a dictionary.
   • Optionally, log the results using the logging level specified in the config.
   • Then return the dictionary to be used downstream (e.g., printing or saving the result).

──────────────────────────────
Integration Details and Considerations

• Forward Pass:
  – Since FOA’s adaptation does not require backpropagation, the Evaluation module makes use of torch.no_grad() for efficiency.
  – The ModelWrapper’s forward() method is expected to incorporate any prompt insertion and activation shifting (thus aligning with the adapted state).

• Consistent Data Processing:
  – The same preprocessing pipeline (as defined in dataset_loader.py) is used to ensure consistency between training (or adaptation) and evaluation.
  – Ground truth labels from the test_loader are used solely for evaluation metrics and do not affect the forward pass.

• Use of Configuration:
  – Configuration parameters such as the batch size, evaluation metrics list, and logging details (from the config.yaml) are used to ensure that the evaluation procedure adheres exactly to the experimental setup described in the paper.
  – For example, while the adaptation module uses a batch size of 64 by default, the Evaluation module simply iterates over the provided test_loader without altering the batch structure.

• Expected Calibration Error Calculation:
  – The ECE computation is key for assessing trustworthiness; careful implementation is needed to ensure that the bins are correctly defined and that the weighting (by the number of samples per bin) is correctly applied.
  – Document any design choice (e.g., n_bins = 15) in the code so that it can be later adjusted in sensitivity studies as described in the research paper.

──────────────────────────────
Summary

The Evaluation class in evaluation.py plays a vital role in quantifying the performance of the FOA adaptation method. It performs forward passes over the test data using the modified (adapted) model, aggregates the outputs, computes both accuracy and calibration error (ECE), and finally returns these metrics for comparison with baseline methods. This design closely aligns with the experimental evaluation criteria described in the paper while using only forward passes (in line with the FOA methodology).

This clear, modular design ensures that:
  – The evaluation process is efficient (using torch.no_grad()).
  – The accuracy and ECE metrics are computed correctly and reproducibly.
  – Any design choices (such as the number of bins in ECE) are documented and can be tuned as needed.

This logic analysis provides a structured plan for writing the Evaluation module code, ensuring that it complies with the proposed design and the experimental setup outlined in both the paper and the config file. 86%|████████▌ | 6/7 [03:07<00:33, 33.28s/it]

[ANALYSIS] main.py
============================================
Below is the detailed logic analysis for main.py. The purpose of main.py is to serve as the entry point of the application. Its responsibilities include loading the configuration (from config.py/config.yaml), initializing the data loaders (via DatasetLoader), setting up the model (using ModelWrapper to load ViT-Base with prompt insertion), creating the CMA-ES optimizer (using CMAOptimizer) and the Trainer (which integrates the forward-only adaptation process, including the fitness function and activation shifting), and finally running an Evaluation module that computes metrics such as accuracy and ECE. The following analysis explains the planned flow and dependencies in main.py:

──────────────────────────────
1. Load the Configuration

• At startup, main.py will import the configuration parameters from config.py. These include:
  – Training parameters: batch size, number of prompt tokens, population_size, lambda_trade-off for ImageNet-C/V2/Sketch (and for ImageNet-R), activation shifting step size (gamma), EMA factor for updating test batch statistics, and the number of source samples.
  – Model parameters: the model name ("ViT-Base"), precision, and whether quantization is enabled.
  – Adaptation settings: whether to use the back-to-source activation shifting mechanism and the update_interval.
  – Optimizer settings: type (CMA-ES) and CMA-ES specific parameters (initial sigma, maximum iterations, etc).
  – Evaluation metrics and logging level.

This configuration will be passed on as a dictionary that will be referenced by all subsequent modules.

──────────────────────────────
2. Initialize the DatasetLoader

• Instantiate a DatasetLoader with the loaded configuration.
  – Call its load_data() method to return:
       • A DataLoader with a small source dataset (e.g., 32 images) used for computing the in-distribution (ID) statistics.
       • A DataLoader for the primary test dataset (the ImageNet-C data).
       • Optionally, additional DataLoaders for the secondary test domains (ImageNet-R, ImageNet-V2, ImageNet-Sketch).

• Use the source dataset to later compute the CLS token statistics (mean and standard deviation) that will be stored in a dictionary (source_stats).

──────────────────────────────
3. Initialize the Model

• Instantiate the ModelWrapper by passing the appropriate parameters (from the configuration). This module is responsible for:
  – Loading the pre-trained ViT-Base model from timm.
  – Injecting the learnable prompt tokens (with count set by training.prompt_embeddings) into the input; these prompts are initialized uniformly (using the specified prompt_init_method).
  – Providing methods to perform a regular forward pass, extract the final-layer CLS tokens (get_cls_tokens), insert candidate prompts (insert_prompts), and perform activation shifting on obtained CLS tokens (activation_shift).

• Immediately after initialization, it is ideal to perform a “dry run” on the source dataset to compute {μ_S, σ_S} – the ID statistics. These values will be used both for the fitness function (to compute activation discrepancy) as well as the activation shifting procedure later on.

──────────────────────────────
4. Set Up the CMA-ES Optimizer

• Create an instance of CMAOptimizer.
  – Its constructor requires knowing the flattened prompt dimension (prompt_dim), which can be derived from the shape of the inserted prompt tokens.
  – Set the population_size to the value from config (training.population_size) and use the CMA-ES parameters provided (such as sigma0 and max_iter).
  
• The optimizer interface includes:
  – sample_candidates(): to generate a list of candidate prompt embeddings.
  – update(): to update the CMA-ES search distribution using the fitness scores from evaluated candidates.
  – get_best_candidate(): to retrieve the best candidate prompt after the CMA-ES update.

──────────────────────────────
5. Initialize the Trainer Module

• Instantiate the Trainer, passing in:
  – The ModelWrapper instance.
  – The CMAOptimizer instance.
  – The configuration.
  
• The Trainer is responsible for running the adaptation process for each test batch. For each test batch:
  – It uses CMAOptimizer.sample_candidates() to generate K candidate prompts.
  – For each candidate prompt, it calls ModelWrapper.insert_prompts() to modify the test inputs.
  – It then runs a forward pass to obtain both the predictions and the CLS token activations.
  – It computes the fitness value for each candidate by combining:
       a. Prediction Entropy – computed over the softmax outputs.
       b. Activation Discrepancy – computed as an L2 loss (or another appropriate divergence) between the test batch’s CLS token statistics and the precomputed source statistics.
     – The trade-off between these two terms uses the lambda parameter from the config (lambda_imagenet or lambda_imagenet_r, scaled by batch size).
  – The CMA-ES algorithm is then run (up to config.optimizer.cma_params.max_iter iterations) to update its internal parameters; finally, the best candidate prompt is then adopted.
  – If config.adaptation.use_activation_shifting is True, the Trainer will additionally call ModelWrapper.activation_shift() to adjust the final-layer CLS tokens:
       • It uses the batch's mean activation (updated with an EMA using config.ema_factor) and shifts the CLS token using the formula: e'_CLS = e_CLS + (activation_shifting_step_size × (μ_S – μ_test)).
  
• If the update_interval (adaptation.update_interval) is set to a value greater than 1 (e.g., for FOA-I with BS=1), the Trainer must buffer the required test samples until the interval is complete, then perform the update.

──────────────────────────────
6. Start the Adaptation / Training Process

• The main.py file will now instruct the Trainer to begin processing the test DataLoader.
  – This loop iterates over each batch from the test DataLoader.
  – For each batch, the adaptation as described above is executed:
       a. Generate candidate prompts.
       b. Evaluate the candidates through forward passes.
       c. Update the CMA-ES distribution.
       d. Insert the best prompt.
       e. Apply activation shifting.
  – Once completed over all test batches, the adaptation process is considered complete.
  
• Although “training” here does not involve backprop, it indicates the online adaptation process.

──────────────────────────────
7. Evaluate the Adapted Model

• After adaptation, instantiate the Evaluation module with:
  – The ModelWrapper (now with the adapted prompt integrated in its pipeline),
  – The test DataLoader (or a separate evaluation DataLoader), and
  – The configuration.
  
• Call the evaluation.evaluate() method to perform forward passes over the entire test dataset.
  – This method computes:
       • Classification accuracy by comparing the model predictions to provided ground-truth labels.
       • Expected Calibration Error (ECE) using, for instance, the scikit-learn utilities and the provided metric functions.

• The resulting metrics are then logged and optionally saved if config.logging.save_results is true.

──────────────────────────────
8. Logging & Finalizing

• Throughout the process (data loading, adaptation, and evaluation), progress is logged using the logging level from config.logging.level.
• The final evaluation metrics (accuracy and ECE) are printed to the console and/or saved to a results file.
• Any exceptions, intermediate results (like fitness evolution examples), and debugging information should be logged to help track the adaptation behavior.

──────────────────────────────
9. Summary of Flow (Sequence Diagram Perspective)

a. main.py starts and loads configuration from config.py/config.yaml.
b. DatasetLoader loads the source dataset (to compute ID statistics) and the test datasets.
c. ModelWrapper is instantiated and a forward pass on the source samples computes μ_S and σ_S.
d. CMAOptimizer is set up using the prompt dimensions and population size.
e. Trainer is initialized with ModelWrapper, CMAOptimizer, and config.
f. For each test batch:
   • Trainer samples candidate prompts.
   • Each candidate is inserted (via ModelWrapper) and evaluated (computing prediction entropy and activation discrepancy as fitness).
   • CMAOptimizer updates the candidate distribution, and the best prompt is selected.
   • Activation shifting is applied to the CLS tokens before prediction.
g. After adaptation, Evaluation runs a full forward pass on test data and calculates accuracy and ECE.
h. The metrics are logged and the process terminates.

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion

The logic in main.py carefully orchestrates the entire forward-only test-time adaptation framework. It integrates configuration parsing, data loading, model initialization with prompt insertion, CMA-ES based prompt adaptation, and activation shifting, and then evaluates the adapted model using the specified metrics. This clear and modular execution plan ensures fidelity to the FOA method described in the paper while complying with the design and configuration settings given.

This analysis sets a solid foundation for writing the actual code.100%|██████████| 7/7 [03:33<00:00, 30.91s/it]100%|██████████| 7/7 [03:33<00:00, 30.52s/it]
