[ANALYSIS] dataset_loader.py
============================================
Below is a detailed logic analysis for the DatasetLoader class in dataset_loader.py:

──────────────────────────────
Overview:
• The DatasetLoader is responsible for reading the experiment configuration (from config.yaml) and then generating synthetic simulation data using functions provided by simulators.py. It targets the benchmark tasks (e.g., Gaussian Linear, Two Moons, SLCP, Lotka–Volterra, SIRD, Hodgkin–Huxley) by calling the corresponding simulation functions. The output of load_data() is a structured dictionary where each key corresponds to a simulation task and each value is itself a dictionary containing at least the (θ, x) pairs plus a label indicating the task type.

──────────────────────────────
Initialization:
1. In the __init__(config: dict) method:
  – Store the entire configuration dictionary locally (e.g., self.config).
  – Extract important parameters such as the simulation budget (found under config["simulator"]["simulation_budget"]) and the random seed (config["seed"]) if needed.
  – Optionally, if the configuration or external arguments specify a particular task (or list of tasks) to simulate, record that; otherwise, prepare to load a default set of tasks.

──────────────────────────────
Data Loading Method (load_data):
2. Define the load_data() method which will execute the following steps:

  a. Read the simulation budget:
   – Use the value from self.config["simulator"]["simulation_budget"] to determine the number of simulations to generate per task.
   – Optionally confirm that the value is a valid integer.

  b. Determine the list of tasks:
   – If the configuration specifies a task (or a list of tasks), use those; if not, default to a set of tasks such as:
    • "gaussian_linear"
    • "two_moons"
    • "slcp"
    • "lotka_volterra"
    • "sird"
    • "hodgkin_huxley"
   – This makes the DatasetLoader extendable later if new tasks are added.

  c. For each task:
   – Import and call the corresponding simulation function from simulators.py. For example,
    • For "gaussian_linear", call: simulate_gaussian_linear(simulation_budget)
    • For "two_moons", call: simulate_two_moons(simulation_budget)
    • For "slcp", call: simulate_slcp(simulation_budget)
    • For "lotka_volterra", call: simulate_lotka_volterra(simulation_budget)
    • For "sird", call: simulate_sird(simulation_budget)
    • For "hodgkin_huxley", call: simulate_hodgkin_huxley(simulation_budget)
   – Ensure that each simulation function returns a dictionary or tuple containing the raw outputs – typically, a set/array for parameters (θ) and a corresponding set/array for data (x).

  d. Data Structuring:
   – For each task, package the returned simulation data into a nested dictionary with keys such as "theta", "x", and "label". The "label" is a string that identifies the task (for instance, "gaussian_linear").
   – You might also record additional metadata (like the simulation budget used or any task-specific notes) within the structured dictionary if needed.

  e. Aggregation:
   – Combine the dictionaries for each task into a single dictionary that is returned by load_data(). The result might look like:
    {
     "gaussian_linear": {"theta": <data>, "x": <data>, "label": "gaussian_linear"},
     "two_moons": {"theta": <data>, "x": <data>, "label": "two_moons"},
     ... etc.
    }
  – This provides a uniform interface to access simulation outputs from later modules (such as tokenization, training, and evaluation).

──────────────────────────────
Error Handling and Logging:
3. Throughout load_data():
  – Log the start of simulation for each task (using the log_interval specified in the configuration if needed).
  – Catch exceptions from the simulation calls (e.g., if a simulation function is not implemented or returns malformed data) and log appropriate error messages.
  – Optionally, enforce the random seed (from config["seed"]) to ensure reproducibility of simulation outputs.

──────────────────────────────
Extensibility:
4. The design allows new simulator functions to be added in simulators.py. The DatasetLoader can be updated to include new tasks simply by adding the corresponding simulation function call and updating the dictionary key.
  – This ensures the DatasetLoader remains modular and adaptable as the codebase grows.

──────────────────────────────
Return Value:
5. Finally, load_data() returns the comprehensive dictionary holding simulation data for each task, fully structured for downstream processing (tokenization, training, and evaluation).

──────────────────────────────
Summary:
• The DatasetLoader class is the central hub for reading configuration parameters from config.yaml and generating synthetic simulation data.
• It leverages simulators.py to generate (θ, x) pairs for benchmark tasks.
• The load_data() method produces a structured dictionary with clear labels, facilitating later stages in the pipeline.
• Key configurable elements (simulation budget, seed, and task selection) are read directly from the provided config dictionary, thus anchoring the reproducibility of the experiments.

This thorough logic analysis ensures that when writing dataset_loader.py, each step—from configuration parsing to data structuring and error handling—is clearly defined and easily integrable with the rest of the modules in the system.

[ANALYSIS] simulators.py
============================================
Below is a detailed logic analysis for the simulators.py module. This module is responsible for generating synthetic simulation data for all benchmark tasks as described in the Simformer paper. Its functions will be invoked by the DatasetLoader. The module must provide one main entry function that dispatches to task‐specific simulation routines (e.g., simulate_gaussian_linear, simulate_two_moons, simulate_lotka_volterra, etc.) based on a task identifier. Each simulation function returns a dictionary containing the generated (θ, x) pairs (and, if needed, additional labels or time information) that will later be tokenized by the Tokenizer.

──────────────────────────────
1. Overall Structure

• A main function (e.g., simulate(task: str, num_simulations: int)) will select the appropriate simulation function based on the task string provided.
• Each simulation sub-function will implement the logic for one task:
  – “gaussian_linear”
  – “gaussian_mixture”
  – “two_moons”
  – “slcp”
  – “tree”
  – “hmm”
  – “lotka_volterra”
  – “sird”
  – “hodgkin_huxley”
• The output of each function will be a dictionary containing at least two keys:
  – "theta": the set of simulator parameters generated (as a NumPy array)
  – "x": the corresponding simulator outputs
• When relevant, the functions will also output additional information (such as simulation time points, when dealing with ODEs) to support irregular or unstructured observations.

──────────────────────────────
2. Task‐Specific Simulation Details

A. Gaussian Linear Simulation
 • Sampling:
  – θ is sampled from a multivariate Gaussian N(0, 0.1 I) (with dimension 10).
  – x is sampled conditionally from N(θ, 0.1 I).
 • Implementation details:
  – Use vectorized numpy.random.normal calls to sample θ (shape: [num_simulations, 10]) and then x (shape: [num_simulations, 10]).
  – Return a dict: { "theta": theta_array, "x": x_array }.

B. Gaussian Mixture Simulation
 • Sampling:
  – θ is sampled from a Uniform distribution U(–10, 10) in ℝ².
  – For each θ, x is drawn from a mixture distribution:
   ○ With probability 0.5, sample x ~ N(θ, I) (standard deviation 1).
   ○ With probability 0.5, sample x ~ N(θ, 0.01 I) (std ≈ 0.1).
 • Implementation details:
  – Generate θ as an array of shape [num_simulations, 2].
  – For each simulation, decide randomly (with probability 0.5 per sample) which Gaussian to use.
  – Use numpy.random.choice or a Bernoulli trial for the mixture decision.
  – Return a dict with the generated θ and x.

C. Two Moons Simulation
 • Sampling:
  – θ is sampled uniformly (typically in ℝ² with each component in [–1, 1]). (The paper emphasizes handling multimodality.)
  – x is generated via a nonlinear transformation of θ to induce a two‐moons shape.
 • Possible approach:
  – Use the first coordinate of θ (scaled by π) as an “angle” and use the sign of the second coordinate (or an additional binary indicator) to choose between two half-circular trajectories.
  – For one “moon,” x = (cos(angle), sin(angle)); for the other, x = (cos(angle), –sin(angle)). Add a small amount of Gaussian noise (e.g., 0.1) to both coordinates.
 • Implementation details:
  – Ensure that the mapping from θ to x is nonlinear so that the posterior is multimodal.
  – Return a dict with θ and x as arrays of appropriate shape (e.g., both as [num_simulations, 2]).

D. SLCP (Simple Likelihood Complex Posterior) Simulation
 • Sampling:
  – θ is sampled uniformly from U(–3, 3) (typically one-dimensional or low-dimensional).
  – x is a 4-dimensional observation generated from θ via a complex nonlinear mapping.
 • Possible implementation:
  – Define a nonlinear function μ(θ) that maps θ to a 4-dimensional mean. For example:
    x[0] = θ + noise,
    x[1] = θ² + noise,
    x[2] = sin(θ) + noise,
    x[3] = cos(θ) + noise,
  – Alternatively, use a multivariate Gaussian N(μ(θ), Σ) with a fixed diagonal covariance (e.g., 0.1² on each axis).
 • Implementation details:
  – Use numpy.random.uniform for θ and numpy.random.normal (with different offsets) for x.
  – Return the generated θ and x.

E. Tree Simulation
 • Sampling:
  – Use a hierarchical (tree-structured) model:
   ○ Sample θ₀ ∼ N(0, 1).
   ○ Sample θ₁ ∼ N(θ₀, 1) and θ₂ ∼ N(θ₀, 1).
  – Observable data x is generated from the latent parameters:
   ○ For example, x₀ ∼ N(sin(θ₁)², 0.2²),
   ○ x₁ ∼ N(0.1 * θ₂, 0.2²),
   ○ x₂ ∼ N(0.1 * θ₂, 0.6²),
   ○ x₃ ∼ N(cos(θ₂)², 0.1²).
 • Implementation details:
  – Generate the three θ values per simulation and then compute x from these values.
  – Return θ as a vector (e.g., [θ₀, θ₁, θ₂]) and x as a vector of four observations.

F. HMM (Hidden Markov Model) Simulation
 • Sampling:
  – Generate a sequential latent state:
   ○ θ₀ ∼ N(0, 0.5²),
   ○ For i = 0,…,9, sample θ₍ᵢ₊₁₎ ∼ N(θᵢ, 0.5²).
  – For each latent state, generate observation xᵢ ∼ N(f(θᵢ), 0.5²). The paper suggests a nonlinear factor like using θᵢ².
 • Implementation details:
  – Produce θ as a vector of length 10 per simulation and similarly for x.
  – Use a loop (or vectorized recurrence if possible) to generate the Markov chain.
  – Return a dict with θ and x (each of shape [num_simulations, 10]).

G. Lotka–Volterra Simulation (Ecology)
 • Sampling (ODE-based):
  – The Lotka–Volterra system involves two state variables (prey and predator) with parameters (α, β, γ, δ).
  – Sample the four parameters from a prior (the paper suggests a “sigmoid-transformed Normal” scaled within a plausible range, e.g., roughly between 1 and 3). In the absence of fully specified distributions, one may sample each parameter from a uniform or normally distributed value and then transform accordingly.
  – Fix initial populations for prey and predator.
 • ODE Integration:
  – Define the Lotka–Volterra differential equations:
   dx/dt = α * x – β * x * y
   dy/dt = δ * x * y – γ * y
  – Use an ODE integrator (e.g., a Runge–Kutta method provided by scipy.integrate.odeint or a custom Euler solver).
 • Observations:
  – After integrating over a given time horizon, select a few time points at random (irregularly spaced) from the solution.
  – Add Gaussian noise (with σ = 0.1) to simulate measurement noise.
 • Return:
  – θ: the vector containing the four parameters.
  – x: a set of observations. This could be structured as a time series (or a list of observations) with both prey and predator measurements.
  – It might be useful to include the observation times in the returned dictionary.

H. SIRD Simulation (Epidemiology)
 • Sampling (ODE-based):
  – The SIRD model includes compartments: Susceptible (S), Infected (I), Recovered (R) and Deceased (D).
  – Parameters:
   ○ Global parameters such as recovery rate γ and death rate μ are sampled (e.g., from Uniform(0, 0.5)).
   ○ The contact rate β(t) is time dependent. Although the paper mentions sampling β from a Gaussian process prior (with an RBF kernel) and then applying a sigmoid transformation, a simplified approach might generate a time series for β(t) that varies over time.
 • ODE Integration:
  – Define the SIRD equations:
   dS/dt = –β(t) * S * I  
   dI/dt = β(t) * S * I – γ * I – μ * I  
   dR/dt = γ * I  
   dD/dt = μ * I  
  – Integrate these equations over a time horizon.
 • Observations:
  – Simulate measurements (potentially with log-normal noise with σ = 0.05) at irregularly chosen time points.
 • Return:
  – θ: the sampled global parameters and a representation of β(t) (or summary statistics for the time-dependent parameter),
  – x: the observed time series or a set of selected observations for S, I, R, D.

I. Hodgkin–Huxley Simulation (Neuroscience)
 • Sampling (ODE-based):
  – The Hodgkin–Huxley model simulates the neuronal membrane voltage and gating variables (e.g., m, h, n) and uses 7 parameters (including maximum conductances, reversal potentials, etc.).
  – Fixed initial conditions (e.g., V₀ = –65 mV) and simulation duration (200 ms) with an external current injection (4 mA) applied between 50 and 150 ms are specified.
 • ODE Integration:
  – Define the Hodgkin–Huxley differential equations as provided in the paper.
  – Use an ODE solver (e.g., Runge–Kutta or Euler method) to simulate the voltage trace and gating variables.
 • Post-processing:
  – The raw simulation outputs (voltage trace) are typically reduced to summary statistics (e.g., peak voltage, width, timing, energy consumption computed from sodium charge).
  – Additionally, include the metabolic energy consumption as an extra summary statistic.
 • Return:
  – θ: the set of simulator parameters (the 7 parameters),
  – x: the computed summary statistics (or optionally the full voltage trace along with the energy value) that will later be compared to MCMC-generated reference posteriors.
  – It is important that these outputs match the format expected by downstream modules.

──────────────────────────────
3. Integration with Configuration

• The simulation budget (number of simulations) is specified in config.yaml under simulator.simulation_budget. The main simulate() function should use this value when generating data.
• Although specific distribution parameters (such as noise levels, ODE time horizons, or number of observation points) are provided in the paper’s description, the code should eventually allow these values to be overridden by configuration if needed.
• For ODE-based simulators, the integration time domain and noise parameters (e.g., σ for Gaussian observation noise) should be hard-coded according to the paper’s specifications unless a config override is provided.

──────────────────────────────
4. Error Handling and Return Format

• Each simulation function should validate the input “num_simulations” (e.g., ensuring it is a positive integer).
• If a task string is not recognized by the main simulate() dispatcher, a meaningful error message should be thrown.
• All functions must return a dictionary in a consistent format so that DatasetLoader can uniformly process the simulation results.
• Consider logging the number of generated simulations and any warnings (e.g., if irregular time points are generated for ODE-based simulations).

──────────────────────────────
5. Final Notes

• Although some functions (especially those using ODE integration) might require more detailed numerical solvers (e.g., Runge–Kutta), the current logic analysis focuses on the core simulation logic as specified in the paper.
• The simulators should be modular so that new tasks can be added later without affecting the core DatasetLoader.
• Document each function’s assumptions (e.g., distribution parameters for Gaussian tasks or the choice of noise level in the Lotka–Volterra model) so that later sensitivity analyses or hyperparameter tuning can be conducted.

This analysis serves as a clear roadmap for writing the code in simulators.py, ensuring fidelity to the paper’s methods while maintaining a modular and easily testable design. 33%|███▎      | 3/9 [01:27<03:19, 33.27s/it]

[ANALYSIS] tokenizer.py
============================================
Below is the detailed logic analysis for the Tokenizer module (tokenizer.py):

────────────────────────────────────────────
Overview:
• The Tokenizer class is responsible for converting raw simulation outputs—each (θ, x) pair along with an associated condition mask M_C—into a sequence of tokens that will serve as input to the transformer-based score network. Each token must encapsulate three pieces of information:
 – An identifier (which tells the model which variable this token represents, e.g. “θ₁”, “x₂”, etc.),
 – A value embedding (which is derived from the raw numerical value), and
 – A condition state (a binary flag indicating whether this variable is observed or unobserved in the current conditioning).
Additionally, for function-valued parameters (when either θ or x is not just a scalar but a function over time or space), the token’s identifier will be enhanced by a random Fourier embedding computed from an index (e.g., the time or spatial coordinate).

────────────────────────────────────────────
Initialization (inside __init__):
1. Read configuration parameters from the provided config dictionary. In particular, use:
 – model.token_dim (expected to be 50) as the overall dimension for each token.
 – Possibly decide on any split between the sub-embeddings (e.g. identifier, value, and condition state). (The exact split is not specified in the paper, so you may define a reasonable partition and document it.)
2. Initialize learned embedding lookup tables (or fixed random vectors) for the variable identifiers:
 – One set for parameters (θ) and one for observations (x). For example, if there are dₜheta tokens and dₓ tokens, you can pre-assign each an embedding vector.
3. Initialize condition state embeddings:
 – Define two embeddings (one for condition state “observed” (flag = 1) and one for “unobserved” (flag = 0)). These will be appended or combined with the other embeddings.
4. (Optional) Initialize Fourier embedding parameters:
 – For function‐valued variables, set up a way to compute random Fourier features (for example, by sampling a random projection matrix and phase offsets). Although the configuration does not explicitly provide “fourier_embedding_dim”, you can decide to use a fixed size (or derive it from token_dim) and document your choice.
5. Store any additional parameters (e.g., random seed from config) that might be useful for reproducibility.

────────────────────────────────────────────
Tokenization Process (inside tokenize method):
Input:
 • theta: a tensor containing parameter values. For a given sample, this might be a 1D array of size dₜheta (or could be higher dimensional if function‐valued).
 • x: a tensor containing the simulator outputs (data observations). Typically a 1D array of size dₓ, but may be function‐valued.
 • condition_mask (M_C): a binary tensor (or a tuple of masks corresponding to theta and x) indicating which tokens are “observed” (1) and which are “unobserved” (0). The mask is resampled externally per training iteration.

Steps:
A. Combine the Variables:
 – Decide on the ordering: by convention, the tokens for all θ are created first followed by tokens for all x.
 – Verify that the length of the condition_mask matches the total number of tokens (θ and x). If not, raise an error.

B. Process Each Token:
 For each element:
  1. Value Embedding:
   – Extract the raw scalar value.
   – Map it to a vector embedding. This can be done via a learned linear projection (or a simple non-linear transformation) that transforms a scalar to a vector of a chosen dimension (e.g. value_embedding_dim). Document the dimensions used so that when later concatenated with the other embeddings, the total matches token_dim.
  2. Identifier Embedding:
   – Determine the “type” of variable: if the element comes from theta, assign an identifier (e.g. “theta_i” with i being the index). For elements from x, use “x_j”.
   – Look up the corresponding identifier embedding. 
   – If the variable is function‑valued (i.e. if its shape indicates multiple indices, such as a time series), then in addition to the base identifier vector, compute a Fourier embedding for the index:
    ○ Use a helper function (e.g. fourier_embedding(index)) that maps the index (or a vector of indices) to a Fourier feature vector.
    ○ Combine (e.g., by addition or concatenation) the base identifier and Fourier embedding to obtain an enhanced identifier embedding.
  3. Condition Embedding:
   – Use the provided condition_mask for the current token. If the mask value is 1 (observed), retrieve the “observed” embedding; if 0 (unobserved), use the “unobserved” embedding.
   – This embedding provides the model with information about which parts of the input are fixed (clean) during diffusion.
  4. Concatenate the Embeddings:
   – Form the final token for that variable by concatenating (or summing, as per design choice) the identifier embedding (possibly enhanced with Fourier features), the value embedding, and the condition embedding.
   – Ensure that the resulting vector’s dimension matches the expected token dimension (50 as per config). If needed, apply a final linear projection to adjust dimensions.

C. Aggregate Tokens:
 – Gather tokens for each variable (first for all parameters then for data) into a list (or stack into a 2D tensor of shape [num_tokens, token_dim]). According to the interface, return a List[Tensor] where each item is one token embedding.
 – Preserve the token order as it will be used by the transformer and later be processed according to the attention masks (M_E).

────────────────────────────────────────────
Output:
• The tokenize(theta, x, condition_mask) method returns a list of token embeddings (each a Tensor) ready to be passed into the transformer’s forward() method.
• The returned tokens encapsulate:
 – The type and positional information (via identifier + Fourier features if applicable),
 – The numeric value information (the embedded value), and
 – The conditioning status (embedded condition flag).

────────────────────────────────────────────
Additional Considerations and Ambiguities:
• Dynamic Attention and Mask Sampling:
 – The exact Bernoulli probability for randomly sampled condition masks is provided in the config as 0.3. However, the tokenizer itself is not responsible for sampling M_C—it is supplied externally. Make sure to document (or assert) that the length and ordering of condition_mask matches the (theta, x) ordering.
• Handling Function-valued Parameters:
 – It is ambiguous how to detect if a variable is function‑valued. A reasonable approach is to check if an element’s shape is not a scalar (e.g., if it has more than one element in its dimension). If so, use the Fourier embedding routine.
 – The details of how to combine Fourier embeddings with identifier embeddings (addition vs. concatenation) may require future tuning.
• Embedding Dimensionality Allocations:
 – The total token dimension (50) must be allocated among the three parts. Establish and document a fixed split (for example, 16 for identifier embedding, 16 for value embedding, and 18 for condition embedding) or use a concatenation followed by a projection layer to ensure the final dimension is correct.
• Reusability:
 – Avoid defining any public member functions beyond the specified ones (i.e. only use __init__ and tokenize) to strictly adhere to the design.
• Random Seed:
 – Use the seed (42 from the config) when initializing any random matrices (e.g. for Fourier features) so that the process is reproducible.

────────────────────────────────────────────
Summary of Flow in tokenize():
1. Receive raw (theta, x) and condition_mask.
2. For each variable (first for each θ element, then for each x element):
 a. Lookup identifier embedding (combine with Fourier features if function‑valued).
 b. Embed the raw scalar value into a value vector.
 c. Retrieve the appropriate condition state embedding based on the corresponding bit in condition_mask.
 d. Concatenate the three embeddings to form the token.
3. Return the list of token vectors as the final tokenized output.

This logic strictly follows the methodology described in the paper and adheres to the design and configuration provided. All configuration parameters (such as token_dim and random_mask_probability) are referenced from the config.yaml, and no extraneous public functions are added. This analysis will guide the implementation of tokenizer.py in our modular system. 44%|████▍     | 4/9 [02:10<03:03, 36.68s/it]

[ANALYSIS] model.py
============================================
Below is a thorough logic analysis for the Model class in model.py. This module is responsible for implementing the transformer‐based score network that, given a tokenized (θ, x) sequence along with a diffusion time input, produces score predictions used in the denoising diffusion objective. The analysis below breaks down the components, how they integrate configuration settings from config.yaml, and the specifics on handling dynamic attention masks.

──────────────────────────────
1. Overall Structure and Responsibilities

• The Model class must:
 – Accept a configuration dictionary (parsed from config.yaml) to set hyperparameters such as token_dim (50), time_embedding_dim (128), number of transformer layers (default 6, with possibility for 8 on complex tasks), attention_heads (4), attention_size (10), widening_factor (3) and hidden_dim (150).
 – Incorporate a diffusion time embedding. This component will map the scalar (or vector) time input into an embedding of dimension time_embedding_dim (128) employing random Fourier features (or a similar strategy). A subsequent linear projection can transform this embedding into a bias (or additive term) that is injected into the token representations.
 – Build the transformer network:
  • A stack of transformer blocks is constructed. Each block consists of a multi-head self-attention component (using the configured number of heads and head size) and a feed-forward network with a widening factor that projects from token_dim to hidden_dim (e.g., 150) and back.
  • Each transformer block should also support residual connections and layer normalization.
  • Most importantly, the self-attention mechanism in each block must accept a dynamic attention mask (M_E) as input. This mask encodes domain-specific dependency structures. If an attention mask is provided (or stored as part of the model state), then each multi-head attention sublayer will use it to restrict or weight the token interactions. If not provided, a dense (all-ones) mask is assumed.
 – Provide a final linear projection layer to map the output of the transformer stack back into the same space as the token embeddings. This final output serves as the score prediction for each token.
 – Expose a forward() method that takes two arguments:
  ◦ A tokenized input – a tensor or list of tensors representing tokens (each token with an identifier embedding, a value embedding, and a binary condition indicator already provided by the Tokenizer).
  ◦ A diffusion time scalar (or vector) t.
  Optionally (or via internal state), dynamic attention masks (M_E) can be applied to the transformer blocks.

──────────────────────────────
2. Detailed Breakdown of Components

A. Diffusion Time Embedding Layer
 • Input: A time value t (typically a scalar for each training sample).
 • Process: Convert t into a high-dimensional embedding using a method such as random Fourier features. This is critical because the diffusion SDE operates over the continuous time domain.
 • Projection: After obtaining the Fourier embedding of t (dimension = 128 as set by time_embedding_dim), it is generally passed through (or added with) a learned linear layer so that its resulting dimension matches the token embedding dimensions (50 or optionally an intermediate dimension) or can be broadcasted to each token.
 • Injection: This processed time embedding is added (or concatenated) with each token in the input sequence. A common approach is to add a bias vector to each token so that the network becomes conditioned on the diffusion time.

B. Transformer Blocks
 • Structure: The transformer network consists of a series of identical layers (number determined by config:model:transformer_layers, default 6).
 • Multi-Head Self-Attention:
  – Each self-attention module takes the token sequence and computes the queries, keys, and values using learned linear projections.
  – The number of attention heads is set (from config:model:attention_heads) and each head’s dimensionality is determined by config:model:attention_size.
  – The attention computation must integrate an attention mask M_E (dynamic) that restricts which tokens can attend to which. For example, if the domain knowledge indicates that some tokens are independent, the mask will zero out those interactions. The implementation should:
   • Accept an attention_mask argument in the forward pass or have it as part of the model state.
   • Apply the mask within the softmax step of the attention mechanism.
 • Feed-Forward Network (FFN):
  – After self-attention (with appropriate residual connection and layer normalization), the tokens are passed through an FFN.
  – The FFN consists of a linear layer that expands the dimension using the widening factor (token_dim * widening_factor = 150 according to the config) followed by a nonlinearity (typically ReLU) and then a linear projection that reduces the dimension back to token_dim.
  – There is also a residual shortcut plus normalization after the FFN.
 • Dynamic Attention Mask Handling:
  – The model must be structured so that each transformer block can be given a (potentially dynamic) attention mask. This mask can change depending on the condition state (M_C) and other dependency structures dictated by domain knowledge.
  – Although not all details about dynamic mask updates are completely fixed, the logic should allow an external caller (e.g., the Trainer or Sampler module) to either supply or update the mask within the forward() call.

C. Final Projection for Score Prediction
 • Once the sequence has been processed by the full transformer, each token now encodes information mixed with the diffusion time.
 • A final linear layer is applied on the token representations to produce the score predictions. The dimension of the score for each token should match the original token’s value dimension.
 • The output tensor shape is [batch_size, sequence_length, token_dim] and will later be used by the Trainer module in computing the denoising score-matching loss (after selective masking using 1 – M_C).

──────────────────────────────
3. Forward() Method Logic

The forward() method should perform the following steps:

Step 1: Input Processing
 • Accept the tokenized input (tokens) and the diffusion time t.
 • Tokens are already in a list or tensor of shape [B, L, token_dim] (B: batch size, L: sequence length).
 • Optionally accept an argument for an attention mask. If none is provided, the model should default to using a dense mask (all ones).

Step 2: Time Embedding
 • Process the diffusion time t through the time embedding module to obtain a time embedding vector of dimension time_embedding_dim.
 • Optionally project or transform the time embedding so that it can be added to each token vector.
 • Broadcast or tile the time embedding across the sequence length and add (or concatenate) it to the token representations.

Step 3: Passing Through Transformer Layers
 • Iterate through the transformer blocks:
  – For each block, use the multi-head self-attention with the dynamic attention mask M_E.
  – Apply the feed-forward network with residual connections.
  – The output of one block becomes the input for the next.
 • Ensure that the attention mask is applied at every block, thereby enforcing the domain-specific dependencies that may have been precomputed or dynamically adjusted.

Step 4: Final Projection
 • Pass the final transformer output (shape [B, L, token_dim]) through a final linear layer to produce the score output.
 • Return the score predictions for each token; these scores will be used during training to compare against the true denoising score ∇_x_t log p_t(x_t|x_0).

──────────────────────────────
4. Configuration and Dynamic Components

• The model’s hyperparameters (e.g., token_dim, number of layers, attention heads, hidden dimensions) must be read from the config dictionary.
• The scaling function s(t) used in guided diffusion is not directly implemented here—it is applied externally during sampling (in sampler.py) when the score output is modified. However, the forward() method must be compatible with such downstream guidance logic.
• The dynamic attention mask (M_E) is a critical element. The design must ensure that:
 – Each transformer block receives an optional mask parameter.
 – The mask can be either dense (default) or structured/directed based on domain knowledge.
 – We document any assumptions regarding the shape (likely [B, L, L] for each batch) and how it is applied within the transformer’s softmax computation.
• Any ambiguities (such as the exact Bernoulli parameters for condition mask sampling and the dynamic update of M_E) are noted for future clarification, but here the model will simply allow the caller to pass in an externally computed mask.

──────────────────────────────
5. Summary of Key Steps in the Code

• In __init__:
 – Parse the model and diffusion configuration (token_dim, time_embedding_dim, number of layers, etc.).
 – Initialize the time embedding submodule.
 – Create a sequence (or list) of transformer blocks, each built with multi-head self-attention (supporting an external attention mask) and feed-forward sublayers.
 – Define a final linear layer for mapping transformer outputs to score predictions.

• In forward():
 – Receive tokenized input and diffusion time.
 – Compute and inject time embedding into the tokens.
 – Process tokens sequentially through transformer layers with the dynamic attention mask applied.
 – Apply the final linear projection and return the score tensor.

By following this logic analysis, one will implement a Model class that accurately represents the transformer-based score network described in the Simformer paper. The design stays faithful to the paper’s methodology and uses configuration parameters from config.yaml, while also incorporating support for dynamic attention masking—a key element for encoding dependency structures in the simulator. 56%|█████▌    | 5/9 [02:43<02:22, 35.60s/it]

[ANALYSIS] trainer.py
============================================
Logic Analysis for Trainer class in trainer.py

Overview:
 The Trainer class is responsible for orchestrating the training loop using a denoising score matching objective on simulated (θ, x) data. During each training iteration, the Trainer must:
  • Sample a condition mask M_C for each (θ, x) pair (selecting one among “joint,” “posterior,” “likelihood,” or “random” based on the configuration).
  • Draw a noise level t uniformly from the configured time interval ([1e-5, 1.0] as provided in config.sde.time_range).
  • Generate a “noisy” version of the input data by first using an SDE-based noise function to compute x_t from the original clean data x₀ and then combining it with x₀ via the condition mask: 
    x_M_C^t = M_C ⊙ x₀ + (1 – M_C) ⊙ x_t.
  • Tokenize the (θ, x_M_C^t) pair (with the chosen M_C) using the provided Tokenizer.
  • Pass the tokenized, noised input along with the time value t as a diffusion time embedding into the transformer-based Model (score network) to obtain score predictions.
  • Compute the target score ∇ₓₜ log pₜ(xₜ | x₀) analytically (for a Gaussian noise model, this is typically (x₀ – xₜ) divided by the variance function σ(t)² from the SDE parameters).
  • Calculate the loss by applying the denoising score matching objective only on the unobserved indices (i.e. multiplying the difference by (1 – M_C)) and taking the squared error, averaged over batch and tokens.
  • Utilize an Adam optimizer (using the learning rate from config.training.learning_rate) to update model parameters.
  • Log training metrics (loss values, iteration count) every config.logging.log_interval steps and, if enabled, save model checkpoints per config.logging.checkpoint_interval.
  • Optionally evaluate on a validation set and implement early stopping based on validation loss improvements.

Detailed Steps:

1. Initialization (in __init__):
  • Receive and store the model (an instance of Model), the tokenizer (an instance of Tokenizer), and the dataset (a dict containing training [and possibly validation] (θ, x) pairs) along with the configuration (from config.yaml).
  • Initialize the Adam optimizer using the learning rate from config.training.learning_rate.
  • Optionally, set aside a validation split from the dataset if early stopping is to be implemented.
  • Set counters and bookkeeping variables (e.g., current epoch, best validation loss, patience counter).

2. Condition Mask Sampling:
  • For each mini-batch, sample a condition mask M_C for each (θ, x) pair.
  • The mask can be one of several types:
   – “joint” mask: typically all zeros (meaning none of the components are observed).
   – “posterior” mask: where data tokens (x) are flagged as observed (1) and parameter tokens (θ) as unobserved (0).
   – “likelihood” mask: the inverse of the posterior mask.
   – “random” mask: drawn element‐wise from a Bernoulli distribution using the probability specified by config.condition_mask.random_mask_probability (here 0.3).
  • The Trainer should randomly select one of these mask types for each batch according to a uniform distribution over config.condition_mask.mask_types.

3. Sampling Diffusion Time and Adding Noise:
  • For each mini‐batch, sample a random noise level t uniformly from the interval defined in config.sde.time_range ([1e–5, 1.0]).
  • For each sample, generate a noisy version x_t of the original data x₀. This is done by adding Gaussian noise scaled according to the SDE parameters (using σ_max, σ_min, etc. from config.sde). In many diffusion models the noisy version is computed as:
   x_t = x₀ + σ(t) · ε    with ε ~ 𝒩(0, I)
  • The noisy input to be fed to the model is constructed by “mixing” the original and noisy data over tokens via:
   x_M_C^t = M_C ⊙ x₀ + (1 – M_C) ⊙ x_t
  • This procedure ensures that for tokens marked as “observed” (M_C = 1), the clean values are retained, and for tokens to be denoised (M_C = 0), the random noise is applied.

4. Tokenization:
  • Pass the (θ, x_M_C^t) along with the condition mask M_C to the Tokenizer using its tokenize() method.
  • The tokenizer converts the data into a sequence of tokens containing:
   – Identifier embeddings (indicating variable identity)
   – Value embeddings (for the numeric values from the simulated data)
   – Condition state indicators (derived from M_C).
  • In cases with function-valued parameters, the tokenizer will also add random Fourier embeddings for indices.
  • The output token sequence is then passed to the model.

5. Forward Pass – Model Prediction:
  • Provide the tokenized sequence and the time value t (which will be embedded into a diffusion time embedding using the model’s internal mechanism) to the Model’s forward() method.
  • The model processes the inputs through its transformer layers (with dynamic attention masks M_E as configured) and outputs a score prediction tensor. This tensor is expected to have the same shape and token-aligned structure as the underlying data (or at least corresponding values).

6. Target Score Computation:
  • Compute the analytical target score ∇ₓₜ log pₜ(xₜ | x₀) for each sample.
  • For a standard Gaussian perturbation, the target score is typically given by:
   target_score = (x₀ – x_t) / σ²(t)    
  where σ²(t) is derived from the diffusion schedule (using parameters sigma_max, sigma_min, etc.). Although the exact formulation depends on the chosen SDE (VESDE in this case), the Trainer must compute this either directly or call a helper function.
  • Ensure that the target score is computed for all tokens corresponding to “unobserved” parts (while tokens flagged as observed should ideally not contribute to the loss).

7. Loss Computation:
  • Compute the difference between the model’s output score and the target score for each token.
  • Apply the elementwise multiplication with (1 – M_C) so that the loss is only computed for the unobserved components.
  • Use the mean squared error (MSE) loss over all tokens and over the batch:
   loss = mean( ((1 – M_C) ⊙ (model_output – target_score))² )
  • Optionally, include a weighting function λ(t) if specified (though the paper leaves this as a general positive weighting).

8. Backpropagation and Optimizer Update:
  • Use the computed loss to perform backpropagation.
  • Update the model parameters using the Adam optimizer with the learning rate from config.training.learning_rate.
  • Given that the system is likely implemented in JAX (or alternatively in PyTorch), care must be taken to ensure that the gradient update is consistent with the framework’s best practices (e.g., functional updates for JAX).

9. Logging, Checkpointing, and Early Stopping:
  • Every config.logging.log_interval steps, log training metrics including the current loss and possibly additional statistics (e.g., norm of gradients or score predictions).
  • If enabled by config.logging.save_checkpoints, save the model checkpoint every config.logging.checkpoint_interval iterations.
  • Periodically (e.g., after each epoch), evaluate the model on a separate validation set to compute the validation loss.
  • Implement an early stopping mechanism that stops training if the validation loss does not improve over a predetermined number of epochs (patience parameter can be added as needed).

10. Finalization:
  • Once training completes (either reaching the maximum number of epochs defined in config.training.epochs or triggering early stopping), finalize the model training.
  • The Trainer should then hand over control to the Sampler/Evaluation modules or save the final trained model for later inference.

Ambiguities / Unclear Points:
  • The exact algorithm for sampling and dynamically updating the attention mask M_E (used inside the Model) is handled by the Model module; however, the Trainer should simply assume that any given tokenized input comes with the proper condition mask M_C.
  • The precise formulation of the scaling function for noise levels σ(t) and the analytic form of ∇ₓₜ log pₜ(xₜ|x₀) given VESDE is not fully specified here. The Trainer should either incorporate a helper utility consistent with the paper’s method or be configurable accordingly.
  • The details on how to split data into training and validation subsets for early stopping might require further clarification from the broader experiment setup.

Summary:
The Trainer class in trainer.py will be implemented as a modular training loop that integrates data loading, condition mask sampling, noisy data generation according to diffusion SDE dynamics, tokenization, forward propagation through a transformer-based score network, loss computation via denoising score matching (restricted to unobserved tokens), gradient-based optimization using Adam, and robust logging/checkpointing with early stopping. All hyperparameters and settings (e.g., batch size, learning rate, SDE parameters, random mask probability) will be drawn from the configuration file (config.yaml) ensuring fidelity to the experimental design described in the paper. 67%|██████▋   | 6/9 [03:23<01:50, 36.88s/it]

[ANALYSIS] sampler.py
============================================
Below is a detailed logic analysis for the sampler.py module. This module’s role is to generate arbitrary conditional samples by implementing the reverse diffusion process via Euler–Maruyama discretization. It also supports guided diffusion by injecting an extra score term derived from a user‐provided constraint function. The analysis is organized into several parts covering initialization, the reverse diffusion loop, support for guidance, and handling of conditional (observed) tokens.

──────────────────────────────
1. Initialization and Setup

• The Sampler class will be initialized with:
 – A reference to the trained Model (which implements a forward(tokens, time) method that returns the score estimate sₚ(x, t)).
 – A configuration dictionary (loaded from config.yaml) that provides the diffusion hyperparameters (e.g., sde.sigma_max, sde.sigma_min, sde.beta_min, sde.beta_max, diffusion.sampling_steps) and other relevant settings.
 – Optionally, any additional flags that specify whether to run guided diffusion or not.

• The __init__ method will store:
 – The number of sampling steps (e.g., 500 from config.diffusion.sampling_steps).
 – The SDE type (here, default to “VESDE” as per config.sde.sde_type) and then initialize any functions needed to compute the noise scale g(t) and an (optional) drift function f(x, t). For simplicity in the VESDE case, we assume f(x,t) = 0.

• Additionally, the sampler may define a helper function to compute the time-dependent noise scale and, if required, the scaling function s(t) used for guided diffusion. A common choice is s(t) = 1 / (σ(t)²) (where σ(t) is the effective noise level at time t). The exact form might be adjustable later if clarification is provided.

──────────────────────────────
2. Constructing the Reverse Diffusion Process

The core method, sample(condition: dict) → dict, will implement the following logic:

A. Setting Up Initial Latent State

 – The reverse diffusion process starts at the final time T. For the VESDE, the terminal distribution pₜ (x_T) is approximately a Gaussian noise distribution.
 – Sample x_T from a Gaussian distribution. (For a vector of tokens, sample independently for each element using the noise scale given by σ_max – note that config.sde.sigma_max defines the maximum noise level.)
 – The sampled x_T has the same shape as the tokenized data.

B. Handling Conditions and Observations

 – The condition dictionary (passed as the “condition” argument) can include:
  • A “condition_mask” (M_C) that is a binary tensor indicating which tokens are “observed” (1) and which are “to be diffused” (0).
  • “observed_values” corresponding to the fixed values that should remain unchanged during the reverse diffusion. This supports arbitrary conditional sampling.
  • Optionally, a “constraint” key with a constraint function c(x_t) for guided diffusion.
 – At every reverse diffusion step, the sampler will “clamp” the tokens indicated by the condition mask to the provided observed values (thus ensuring that the observed information remains clean).

C. Setting Up the Time Discretization

 – Create a discretized time grid going from t = T (e.g., t = 1.0 from config.sde.time_range[1]) to t = t_min (e.g., t = 1e-5 from config.sde.time_range[0]). The number of steps is given by config.diffusion.sampling_steps.
 – Compute the time step dt = (T - t_min) / (number_of_steps). Note that the integration will be performed in reverse time.

──────────────────────────────
3. Reverse Diffusion Loop (Euler–Maruyama Discretization)

For each time step in the reverse diffusion (iterating backwards over the time grid):

A. Score Computation
 – Query the trained Model via its forward(tokens, t) method to obtain the denoising score sₚ(x_t, t) for the current latent sample x_t (which is represented in its tokenized form).
 – If a condition is imposed (i.e., the user provided a condition mask), the network is fed the current tokens with the “clean” tokens (observed values) in positions indicated by M_C and the noisy tokens in the others.

B. Incorporating Guided Diffusion
 – If the condition dictionary includes a “constraint” function, the sampler computes an additional gradient term. Specifically, it calculates:
  ∇ₓₜ log σ(– s(t) * c(x_t))
  • Here, σ denotes the sigmoid function.
  • The scaling function s(t) (often chosen as 1/(σ(t)²) or a similar form ensuring s(t) → ∞ as t → 0) is computed using the current time t.
 – The effective score used in the update is then:
  effective_score = sₚ(x_t, t) + guidance_term  (with guidance_term absent if no constraint is provided)
 – This extra term guides the reverse diffusion process toward regions that satisfy the constraint c(x_t) ≤ 0.

C. Euler–Maruyama Update
 – Calculate the noise scale g(t) for the current time step. For a VESDE, g(t) is defined by the SDE parameters (this may be computed using a schedule that interpolates between sigma_min and sigma_max; the exact formulation can be implemented as a helper function).
 – Given that f(x,t) is assumed 0 in VESDE, the reverse SDE update (for a small time step dt) becomes:
  xₜ₋₍dt₎ = xₜ – [g(t)² * effective_score] * dt + g(t) * √(dt) * noise
   • "noise" is sampled from a standard Gaussian with the same shape as x_t.
 – Perform this update for all tokens.

D. Clamping Observed Tokens
 – After each update, enforce the condition by resetting token values at positions where condition_mask is 1 to the fixed observed values.
 – This ensures that during every integration step the “observed” part remains unchanged, as required by the conditional sampling procedure.

E. Iteration
 – The loop continues, updating x_t for each time t in descending order until t reaches the minimum value (near 0).

──────────────────────────────
4. Returning the Final Sample

• Once the reverse diffusion integration is complete (i.e., when t is at the lower bound), the final sample x₀ is obtained.
• Convert (if needed) the tokenized sample back to the simulator’s original (θ, x) format (or return the token sequence; decoding may be handled elsewhere).
• The sample() method then returns a dictionary containing e.g.:
 – The final sample (e.g., under key “sample”),
 – Possibly additional information such as the full time trajectory (if logged) or any metrics collected during sampling.

──────────────────────────────
5. Configurable Options and Ambiguities

• The number of sampling steps (default of 500 per config.yaml) is used for discretization, though experiments indicate that even fewer steps (e.g., 50) can sometimes suffice.
• The exact function for s(t) (the guidance scaling function) is not specified exactly in the paper. A safe default is s(t) = 1/(σ(t)²) – but this may be subject to tuning.
• The implementation of the dynamic attention mask or changes to M_E are not managed within the sampler; the sampler uses the Model’s forward pass, which is assumed to already incorporate such changes.
• The precise method for computing g(t) (or how to interpolate between sigma_min and sigma_max as a function of time t) is ambiguous. A clear helper function can be implemented based on common practices for VESDEs.
• The integration loop must be robust against potential numerical instabilities (e.g., when t → 0, s(t) may grow large). Appropriate clipping or numerical stabilization techniques should be considered.

──────────────────────────────
6. Summary of Key Steps in sampler.py

• __init__(model, config):
 – Save model and configuration.
 – Initialize the discretization grid, SDE parameters, and helper functions for computing g(t) and s(t).

• sample(condition: dict):
 1. Generate initial latent x_T from a standard Gaussian scaled by sigma_max.
 2. Retrieve condition details (condition_mask and observed_values; and optionally constraint function).
 3. For t in the reverse time grid:
  a. Compute model score: score = model.forward(current_tokens, t).
  b. If a constraint exists, compute guidance term = gradient(log sigmoid(– s(t)*c(x_t))).
  c. Compute effective_score = score + (guidance term if present).
  d. Compute noise scale g(t) and sample additive noise.
  e. Perform Euler–Maruyama update: xₜ₋₍dt₎ = xₜ – g(t)² * effective_score * dt + g(t) * sqrt(dt) * noise.
  f. Clamp the tokens that are observed (using condition_mask and observed_values).
 4. Return the final denoised sample along with any auxiliary information.

This completes the logic analysis for sampler.py. All operations strictly adhere to the provided design and configuration parameters from config.yaml, and they mirror the methodology described in the paper while ensuring modular and flexible implementation. 78%|███████▊  | 7/9 [03:58<01:12, 36.19s/it]

[ANALYSIS] evaluation.py
============================================
Below is a detailed logic analysis written for the Evaluation module (evaluation.py). This analysis explains how the Evaluation class is intended to work and its role in quantifying model performance according to the Simformer paper, while respecting the design and configuration file.

──────────────────────────────
Overview:
──────────────────────────────
The Evaluation module is responsible for assessing the performance of the trained transformer-based diffusion model. Its goals are threefold:
 • Compute quantitative metrics such as the Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST) accuracy, the negative log likelihood (NLL) using a probability flow ODE, and calibration via expected coverage.
 • Generate visual plots that compare the model’s output (for example, posterior marginal distributions, joint distributions, and posterior predictives) with reference samples obtained from MCMC (or another “ground truth”) procedure.
 • Provide a summary of evaluation metrics in a structured dictionary for logging and further analysis.

The Evaluation class is initialized with three key inputs:
 1. The trained Model instance (which provides the forward(tokens, time) API representing the transformer-based score network).
 2. The evaluation dataset (a structured dict with simulation data – including (θ, x) pairs and reference condition samples for each task).
 3. The configuration settings loaded from config.yaml (which include diffusion parameters, transformer hyperparameters, and evaluation-specific settings such as batch size or random seeds).

──────────────────────────────
Evaluation Workflow and Interfaces:
──────────────────────────────

1. __init__(self, model, dataset, config)
 • Store references to the model, dataset, and configuration.
 • Optionally, initialize any internal variables such as a random seed (from config.seed) and logging parameters (e.g., log_interval).

2. evaluate(self) → dict
 • This is the main method that orchestrates the evaluation process. It invokes several subroutines to compute different metrics and produce plots. The high-level steps are:
  a. For one or more chosen conditional tasks (for instance, evaluate the posterior, likelihood, and arbitrary conditionals), generate samples using either a separate Sampler instance or by directly calling the model’s forward method on properly tokenized inputs.
  b. For each task:
   – Use the model to produce sample sets from the learned diffusion model. The evaluation should respect the same condition mask (M_C) setting that corresponds to the target conditional (e.g., for posterior inference, data tokens are observed and parameter tokens are unobserved).
   – Retrieve a corresponding reference set of samples obtained via MCMC or an accepted baseline method.
  c. Compute the Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST) accuracy:
   – Train (or use a pre-built) classifier to discriminate between model-generated samples and reference samples.
   – A value near 0.5 indicates that the two sample sets are indistinguishable.
  d. Compute the Negative Log Likelihood (NLL):
   – Evaluate the NLL using the probability flow ODE. This involves taking a set of (θ, x) samples, running a numerical ODE (e.g., using Euler–Maruyama discretization) over the diffusion time interval (using parameters from config.sde and diffusion.time_range), and then computing the log probability.
  e. Compute calibration using expected coverage:
   – For a range of α values (e.g., 10%, 20%, … up to 90%), compute the percentage of cases where the true parameter falls within the highest density region implied by the model’s estimated conditional distribution.
   – The evaluation should compare these empirical coverages against the ideal diagonal (α expected to match empirical frequency).
  f. Generate plots:
   – Posterior Marginals: Plot 1D or pairwise marginal distributions for parameters. Overlap histograms/density estimates from model samples and MCMC references.
   – Joint Distributions: For lower-dimensional tasks (such as Gaussian Linear or Two Moons), draw scatter plots or contour plots comparing the joint density.
   – Posterior Predictives: For tasks like Lotka-Volterra or Hodgkin–Huxley, plot the time series (including uncertainty bands) of the model’s predictions against observed simulator outputs.
  g. Collect all computed metrics (C2ST accuracy, NLL, and coverage values) into a dictionary.
  h. Optionally, save visual plots to disk (using matplotlib) and log metric summaries (using the logging configuration parameters from config.logging).

3. Subroutine Details:
 • compute_c2st(self, model_samples, reference_samples) → float
  – This function takes two sample sets and trains a classifier to distinguish them.
  – The classifier might be a simple logistic regression or a small neural network. The final accuracy is reported (ideally near 0.5 indicates a good match).
  – The random split, cross-validation, and appropriate batching should be controlled by evaluation parameters (e.g., logging intervals can be used to report intermediate accuracy).

 • compute_nll(self, model, tokens, time_grid) → float
  – Using the probability flow ODE technique, this subroutine computes the log-likelihood of the observed tokens.
  – It will use the diffusion hyperparameters from config.sde and config.diffusion.sampling_steps.
  – The integration (likely via Euler–Maruyama or another ODE solver) produces an estimate of the log probability. Note that this is computationally heavier than generating samples.
  – Since transformation is not directly trained on likelihood, slight discrepancies are expected compared with baselines.

 • compute_calibration(self, model_samples, true_parameters, alpha_levels: List[float]) → dict
  – For each α-level, determine the highest density region (HDR) or use quantiles from the estimated density.
  – Calculate the fraction of cases in which the true parameter falls inside the HDR.
  – Return a dictionary mapping each α to the empirical coverage.
  – Generate a calibration plot (expected coverage plot) showing the observed line versus the diagonal.

 • generate_plots(self, model_samples, reference_samples, task_type: str)
  – Based on the task_type, choose appropriate visualization:
   – For low-dimensional parameter spaces, produce 1D or 2D density/histogram plots.
   – For time-series data (as in Lotka-Volterra or Hodgkin-Huxley), produce line plots showing uncertainty bands.
  – Save plots to file and/or display interactively depending on configuration (config.logging.save_checkpoints or specific flags).

──────────────────────────────
Integration with Other Modules:
──────────────────────────────
• The Evaluation class will assume that the model passed to it has a standard forward(tokens, time) method (as defined in model.py) and that tokenized inputs have been generated using the Tokenizer (tokenizer.py). It is therefore important that Evaluation uses the same configuration for condition masks (M_C) as used in training.
• In some cases, Evaluation might also interact with the Sampler class (sampler.py) if generating samples via reverse diffusion is needed for C2ST or posterior predictive checks.
• The Evaluation module uses simulation data from the DatasetLoader (dataset_loader.py) and possibly simulation functions from Simulators (simulators.py) to obtain the ground truth reference samples, which are then compared to the model’s outputs.

──────────────────────────────
Attention to Configuration:
──────────────────────────────
• All hyperparameters such as diffusion SDE parameters (σ_max, σ_min, etc.), the number of sampling steps (500 as defined in config.diffusion), transformer parameters, as well as the batch size and random seed (42) are to be read from the config dictionary.
• The condition mask settings (mask types, and random_mask_probability = 0.3) must be consistently applied if evaluation involves varying conditional configurations.
• The evaluation metrics (log_interval, checkpoint_interval) may be used to control how frequently visualizations and intermediate metrics are logged.

──────────────────────────────
Handling Ambiguities:
──────────────────────────────
• The exact implementation details for computing the probability flow ODE (for NLL) are not fully specified in the paper and may require prototyping and sensitivity analysis. We will document the chosen numerical method and any approximations.
• Similarly, details of the dynamic attention mask updates are not directly used in evaluation but may affect the quality of the output distributions. We assume that the Model and Sampler modules already embed these decisions.
• The classifier for C2ST is not specified explicitly. For evaluation purposes, a simple baseline classifier (such as logistic regression using numpy or a light JAX implementation) should suffice. We need to report the accuracy along with any variability (possibly averaged over multiple runs).

──────────────────────────────
Expected Outcome:
──────────────────────────────
After execution, evaluation.py should produce:
 • A dictionary containing key metrics: “c2st_accuracy”, “nll”, “calibration” (a sub-dictionary with each α-level and its empirical coverage), and maybe other auxiliary metrics.
 • Visual plots saved to disk (e.g., “posterior_marginals.png”, “joint_distribution.png”, “calibration_plot.png”, “posterior_predictives.png”) that clearly illustrate how well the Simformer’s inferred conditional distributions match the reference data.
 • Logging output that tracks progress and highlights any discrepancies between the model outputs and reference simulation benchmarks.
 • A clear summary that reinforces the claims of simulation efficiency and accuracy as described in the paper.

──────────────────────────────
Conclusion:
──────────────────────────────
This logic analysis for evaluation.py outlines the necessary steps and design components required to comprehensively assess the performance and calibration of the trained Simformer model. By breaking down the evaluation into subroutines for C2ST accuracy, NLL computation via the probability flow ODE, and calibration tests with expected coverage, as well as by generating clear visual comparisons between the model and reference MCMC samples, this module serves as the critical measurer of fidelity to the paper’s experimental results. All configurations and hyperparameters are drawn from config.yaml to ensure reproducibility and consistency across experiments.

This detailed plan will guide the eventual implementation of evaluation.py to reliably evaluate and visualize the performance of the proposed all-in-one simulation-based inference method. 89%|████████▉ | 8/9 [04:26<00:33, 33.85s/it]

[ANALYSIS] main.py
============================================
Below is a detailed logic analysis for main.py. This analysis explains, step by step, how the main entry point will use the configuration (from config.yaml) and orchestrate the overall experiment. It describes how main.py will coordinate the data loading, model initialization, training, sampling, and evaluation based on the design and plan.

──────────────────────────────
1. Read and Parse Configuration

• At the very start, main.py uses Hydra (or an equivalent configuration manager) to load config.yaml.  
• The configuration includes settings for training (learning_rate, batch_size, epochs, optimizer), SDE parameters (σ_max, σ_min, β_min, β_max, time_range), transformer/model settings (token_dim, time_embedding_dim, number of layers, attention_heads, widening_factor, hidden_dim), simulator simulation budget, diffusion sampling steps, condition mask parameters (including random_mask_probability), and logging information (log_interval, checkpoint saving).  
• The seed (42) from the configuration is used to initialize both NumPy’s and JAX’s random generators in order to ensure reproducibility.

──────────────────────────────
2. Initialize Core Modules

a. DatasetLoader  
 • Create an instance of the DatasetLoader by passing the loaded configuration.  
 • The DatasetLoader will use the simulator functions (via the Simulators module) according to the desired task/job (e.g., Gaussian Linear, Two Moons, SLCP, Lotka–Volterra, SIRD, Hodgkin–Huxley) and the simulation_budget (100000 simulations) as provided in config.yaml.  
 • Its load_data() method returns a structured dictionary containing the simulation data: pairs of (θ, x) along with any task-specific labels or metadata.

b. Simulators (if needed)  
 • While the DatasetLoader is responsible for data generation, it internally calls functions from the Simulators module.  
 • This module contains ODE-based and stochastic simulation routines to generate synthetic (θ, x) pairs for the benchmark tasks.

c. Tokenizer  
 • Instantiate the Tokenizer with the configuration.  
 • The Tokenizer’s tokenize(theta, x, condition_mask) method is responsible for converting simulator outputs into a sequence of tokens.  
  – Each token will include an identifier embedding (which uniquely labels each variable), a value embedding (the numerical value), and a binary condition indicator (extracted from the condition mask).  
  – For function-valued parameters, it will incorporate a shared base embedding and additional Fourier embeddings.

d. Model  
 • Initialize the Transformer-based score network (Model) with the model parameters from the configuration.  
 • Parameters such as token_dim (50), time_embedding_dim (128), the number of transformer_layers (6 by default, possibly 8 for more complex tasks), attention_heads (4), attention_size, and the hidden_dim (150, computed from widening_factor) are passed to the Model.  
 • The Model’s forward(tokens, time) method will process the token sequence along with a diffusion time embedding and use a dynamic attention mask (M_E) to incorporate domain-specific dependencies.  
 • This module also has the flexibility to adapt to either a dense or a structured (directed/undirected) attention mask as specified in the design.

e. Trainer  
 • Create a Trainer instance by providing the Model, Tokenizer, loaded simulation dataset, and the configuration.  
 • Trainer’s responsibilities include:
  – For each training batch, sampling a condition mask (M_C) from one of the predefined types (“joint”, “posterior”, “likelihood”, or “random”) based on the random_mask_probability from config (here, 0.3).  
  – Sampling a noise level t uniformly from the diffusion time_range [1e–5, 1.0].  
  – Constructing a “noisy” input: using x_M_C^t = (1 – M_C) ⊙ x_t + M_C ⊙ x_0 (i.e. only the unobserved parts receive noise).  
  – Passing the tokenized input through the Model (using its forward method) and computing the denoising score-matching loss.  
  – Optimizing the network via the Adam optimizer (with learning_rate from config) and logging training metrics at intervals (based on log_interval).  
  – Early stopping is triggered using validation loss and checkpoints are saved as configured.

f. Sampler  
 • After training, instantiate the Sampler with the trained Model and configuration settings.  
 • The Sampler implements the reverse diffusion process:
  – It uses Euler–Maruyama discretization over a specified number of diffusion sampling steps (default 500) as set in config.  
  – It supports guided diffusion by optionally adding a guidance term s_φ(x_t, t|c) ≈ s_φ(x_t, t) + ∇_x_t log σ(-s(t) c(x_t)) where the scaling function s(t) (to be determined, e.g. 1/σ(t)²) and a constraint function c(x_t) are provided during sampling.
  – It generates arbitrary conditional samples by fixing the tokens corresponding to the conditioned variables (as set in condition dictionary) and diffusing the remaining tokens.

g. Evaluation  
 • Finally, initialize the Evaluation module by providing the trained Model, the simulation dataset (or part reserved for evaluation) and the configuration.  
 • The Evaluation class is responsible for:
  – Computing metrics like the Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST) accuracy between samples from the Model and reference MCMC samples.
  – Estimating log probabilities using the probability flow ODE, even though the model was not trained directly for likelihood.
  – Generating calibration plots (expected coverage) and visualizations of joint/posterior marginals.
  – Comparing performance against other SBI methods (if such reference data is available).

──────────────────────────────
3. Orchestrate the Experiment (Execution Flow)

a. Configuration and Initialization  
 • main.py begins by reading the configuration and setting up the random seed.  
 • It instantiates the DatasetLoader.  
 • Loads the simulation data (using load_data call) and logs the dataset characteristics.

b. Training Phase  
 • main.py then instantiates the Tokenizer, Model, and Trainer objects.  
 • It calls Trainer.train() to start the training loop.  
 • During training, each batch involves:
  – Sampling a condition mask (M_C) using one of the types (as per the configuration/mask_types) and a Bernoulli process with p=0.3.
  – Sampling diffusion time t and adding selective noise, then tokenizing the input.
  – Forward propagating through the Model and computing the denoising score-matching loss.
  – Backpropagation using the Adam optimizer.
 • Training progress (loss, metrics) is logged at intervals defined in the configuration (log_interval), and checkpoints are saved if enabled.

c. Sampling Phase  
 • Once training is complete, main.py creates an instance of the Sampler.  
 • The Sampler is then used to generate samples for a specified condition (for instance, posterior condition wherein data tokens are observed, and parameter tokens are generated).  
 • Guided diffusion can be applied if additional constraints (such as interval constraints) are required.  
 • The generated samples (posterior, likelihood, etc.) are returned and possibly visualized.

d. Evaluation Phase  
 • The Evaluation module is then instantiated and called to compute quantitative metrics (C2ST accuracy, negative log-likelihood, expected coverage) and generate plots comparing model outputs with reference samples.  
 • Evaluation may also include calibration tests to ensure the quality of the approximation.

e. Logging and Finalization  
 • Throughout the process, main.py will log key events (configuration loading, start/end of training epochs, checkpointing, sampling completion, evaluation results).  
 • The final evaluation metrics and any figures or plots are saved or displayed to allow further analysis.

──────────────────────────────
4. Summary

• main.py serves as the central hub: It reads the configuration, initializes all required modules (DatasetLoader, Simulators via DatasetLoader, Tokenizer, Model, Trainer, Sampler, and Evaluation), and then orchestrates the experiment.  
• It first loads or simulates data, then trains the transformer-based score network via denoising score matching under a diffusion process, and finally runs the reverse diffusion sampler (with or without guided diffusion) to generate arbitrary conditional samples.  
• Evaluation metrics (C2ST, NLL, calibration) are computed and logged, and final results are output to help assess both accuracy and simulation efficiency.

Any ambiguities (e.g., the precise dynamic attention mask update or the scaling function s(t) for guided diffusion) are noted in the code’s documentation/comments and may be refined through experimentation.

This analysis provides a step-by-step outline of the main entry point’s control flow and interactions with other modules, ensuring that our implementation adheres closely to the experimental design and methodology described in the paper.100%|██████████| 9/9 [04:50<00:00, 30.70s/it]100%|██████████| 9/9 [04:50<00:00, 32.28s/it]
