[ANALYSIS] trainer.py
============================================
Below is an in-depth logic analysis for trainer.py. This analysis explains how the Trainer class will implement the training loop, incorporating all elements from the Simformer paper, the overall reproduction plan, the system design, and the configuration settings provided in config.yaml.

──────────────────────────────
1. Overall Responsibility

• The Trainer class is responsible for orchestrating the full training cycle. It must:
 – Sample mini-batches from the simulation dataset (provided by DatasetLoader).
 – Inject noise via the forward SDE process (using the VESDE/VPSDE formulation defined in the config).
 – Sample condition masks for each batch to indicate which tokens are “observed” (M_C) and which are to be denoised.
 – Compute the denoising score matching loss on the unobserved (i.e., flexible) subspace.
 – Backpropagate the loss and update model parameters using an optimizer (Optax’s Adam).
 – Optionally monitor and log metrics (such as the average loss) and check for early stopping based on a validation loss.

──────────────────────────────
2. Initialization (__init__)

• The __init__ method will receive:
 – A Model instance (the transformer-based score model, which already implements forward(x, t, mask) and compute_score).
 – A dataset (the structured dictionary output from DatasetLoader with simulation samples, parameters, observations, etc.).
 – A configuration dictionary (loaded via Hydra from config.yaml) that provides training hyperparameters, SDE parameters, simulation budget, and condition mask specifications.

• In __init__, the Trainer will:
 – Save the references to the model, data, and config.
 – Initialize key training hyperparameters from config.training (batch_size, learning_rate, max_epochs, early_stopping).
 – Store SDE parameters from config.sde (e.g., sigma_max, sigma_min, beta_min, beta_max) and any additional diffusion settings.
 – Set up the optimizer via Optax (using Adam with the learning rate from config).
 – Prepare any necessary random seeds or PRNG keys for JAX (to sample noise, time steps, and condition masks).
 – Optionally, prepare an iterator or index for mini-batch sampling if the dataset is provided as a complete dictionary.

──────────────────────────────
3. Mini-Batch Sampling & Condition Mask Generation

• For each training step, the Trainer must:
 – Extract a mini-batch of “clean” data samples x₀ (and optionally the corresponding simulator parameters) from the dataset.
 – Sample a noise level t uniformly from an interval (for example, in the range [1e-5, 1.0]) for each sample. (The paper uses a uniform draw over t and config.sde does not specify the bounds explicitly beyond the lower bound suggested by [1e-5, 1].)
 – Sample the condition mask M_C for each sample. The mask is a binary vector specifying which tokens are preserved (“observed” = 1) and which are to be “denoised” (0). Based on config.simulation.condition_mask.types (which include "joint", "posterior", "likelihood", "random") and the provided random mask probability (0.3), the Trainer will:
  • Either select a fixed mask type (“joint”, “posterior”, or “likelihood”) or, in the case of “random,” sample from a Bernoulli distribution (p = 0.3) per element.
 – Note: The generated M_C is later used both in the noise injection (to keep observed values clean) and in the loss calculation (to restrict the loss computation to unobserved dimensions).

──────────────────────────────
4. Forward SDE (Noise Injection)

• Based on the SDE type from config.sde.type (default "VESDE"), the Trainer must implement the “forward” noise process for each mini-batch:
 – For VESDE, using parameters sigma_min and sigma_max, compute the noise scale sigma(t). A common formulation is:
   sigma_t = sigma_min * (sigma_max / sigma_min)^t
 – For each sample in the batch:
  • Sample a random noise vector (from a standard normal distribution).
  • Compute xₜ = x₀ + sigma(t) * noise.
 – Then, to enforce the conditional “clean” values for observed tokens, compute:
   x_MCₜ = M_C ⊙ x₀ + (1 - M_C) ⊙ xₜ
   (where “⊙” means elementwise multiplication)
 – This yields the noised input that keeps the observed (conditioned) dimensions intact, as required by the paper.

──────────────────────────────
5. Loss Computation (Denoising Score Matching Objective)

• Once x_MCₜ is computed, the trainer will:
 – Use the model’s method (e.g., model.compute_score(x_MCₜ, t, M_C)) to obtain the predicted score for each token.
 – Compute the ground-truth score ∇ₓₜ log pₜ(xₜ | x₀). For a Gaussian forward process (as is standard in score matching), the target gradient is usually given by:
   target_score = -(xₜ - x₀) / (sigma(t)²)
   (Ensure the sign and scaling match the diffusion formulation.)
 – Multiply the difference between the predicted score and target_score elementwise by (1 - M_C) so that only the unobserved components contribute to the loss.
 – Compute the squared L2 norm of this diffence, sum (or mean) over the token dimensions and the batch.
 – Formally, the loss per sample is:
   ℓ(ϕ, M_C, t, x₀, xₜ) = || (1 - M_C) ⊙ (predicted_score - target_score) ||²
   and then take an expectation (i.e., average over the mini-batch).

──────────────────────────────
6. Parameter Update Using Optax

• With the loss computed, the Trainer must:
 – Use JAX’s automatic differentiation (jax.grad) to compute the gradient of the loss with respect to the model parameters.
 – Use the Optax optimizer (Adam) with the learning rate from config.training.learning_rate to compute parameter updates.
 – Update the model parameters and the optimizer state accordingly.
 – Optionally, the update step can be jitted and vectorized (e.g., via jax.jit and jax.vmap) to accelerate training on accelerators (GPU/TPU).

──────────────────────────────
7. Training Loop & Early Stopping

• The core training loop will:
 – Loop over epochs (up to config.training.max_epochs).
 – In each epoch, iterate through mini-batches until the simulation budget (config.simulation.simulation_budget) is exhausted or the dataset iterator ends.
 – After processing each mini-batch, log or print the training loss and other metrics.
 – Optionally compute validation loss (if a held-out validation set is provided) and check for early stopping (when config.training.early_stopping is true).
 – Save model checkpoints periodically for reproducibility.

──────────────────────────────
8. Integration with Other Modules

• The Trainer class works together with:
 – DatasetLoader: which supplies the simulation data in a structured dictionary containing “parameters”, “observations”, and possibly pre-generated or default masks.
 – Model: which provides the forward pass and score prediction. The Trainer calls model.forward or model.compute_score with arguments (x_MCₜ, t, M_C) so that the transformer can internally handle tokenization and dynamic attention mask (M_E) adjustment.
 – The configuration values (from Hydra via config.yaml) ensure that hyperparameters (batch size, learning rate, SDE settings, transformer dimensions, etc.) are uniformly used.

──────────────────────────────
9. Additional Considerations

• Randomness and Reproducibility:
 – Establish a PRNG key or set of keys at Trainer initialization for sampling time steps, noise, and condition masks.
 – Use JAX random functions consistently to permit reproducibility.
• Modularity & JIT:
 – The update function (loss + gradient computation + optimizer update) should be jitted.
 – The trainer may encapsulate helper utilities (e.g., a function that computes sigma(t) given t and config.sde settings) as internal methods.
• Logging & Checkpointing:
 – Implement logging of loss metrics, and possibly other statistics (like the average norm of gradients) per epoch.
 – Include optional checkpoint saving.
• Extensibility:
 – Design the Trainer so that if another SDE variant (e.g., VPSDE) is selected in config.sde.type, the noise injection and target score calculation functions are easily adjustable.
 – Ensure that the condition mask sampling is flexible, supporting the different types (“joint”, “posterior”, “likelihood”, “random”) as described.

──────────────────────────────
Summary of Core Trainer Logic Flow

1. Initialize the Trainer with the Model instance, dataset (from DatasetLoader), and configuration parameters.
2. For each training mini-batch:
 a. Sample x₀ from the dataset.
 b. Sample a noise level t uniformly (using the bounds defined by the forward SDE).
 c. Sample the condition mask M_C according to the configured mask type and probability.
 d. Compute sigma_t from t and perform noise injection: 
  xₜ = x₀ + sigma(t) * noise.
 e. Build the condition-modified input: x_MCₜ = M_C ⊙ x₀ + (1 - M_C) ⊙ xₜ.
 f. Forward x_MCₜ (with t and M_C) through the Model to predict the score.
 g. Compute the target score as –(xₜ – x₀) / (sigma(t)²).
 h. Compute the loss as the mean squared error over the unobserved components (weighted by (1 - M_C)).
 i. Backpropagate the loss (using jax.grad) and update the model parameters via Optax Adam.
3. Log training metrics, apply early stopping when needed, and update model checkpoints if applicable.
4. Continue until the simulation budget is exhausted or maximum epochs are reached.

This analysis summarizes all key steps and interactions needed in trainer.py. It ensures full reproducibility of the denoising score-matching training and adheres strictly to the paper’s methodology while using the provided config settings and the designed modular interfaces. 50%|█████     | 3/6 [02:59<02:59, 59.92s/it]

[ANALYSIS] evaluation.py
============================================
# Evaluation Module Logic Analysis

This analysis details the planned structure and logic for the Evaluation component (evaluation.py) of our Simformer reproduction. Its purpose is to assess the performance of our trained transformer‐based, score-model diffusion network using multiple metrics (C2ST, negative log likelihood via the probability flow ODE, and calibration via expected coverage) and to generate corresponding visualizations.

──────────────────────────────
1. Overview

• The Evaluation class will be initialized with the trained Model instance, evaluation data (obtained from the DatasetLoader), and the configuration parameters (loaded from config.yaml via Hydra).  
• It will compute quantitative metrics as well as produce qualitative plots.  
• Metrics include:
 – Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST): to measure the similarity between samples drawn from the model (e.g. inferred posteriors) and reference (ground-truth) samples (e.g., obtained using MCMC). A result near 0.5 indicates that the two sets are statistically indistinguishable.  
 – Negative Log Likelihood (NLL): computed using the probability flow Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) formulation to approximate the likelihood of the true data under the model.  
 – Calibration Analysis (Expected Coverage): this verifies that the estimated posterior is well calibrated by checking whether the true parameters fall within the highest density regions at the correct frequency.

• Qualitative visualizations will include:
 – Marginal/posterior density plots (histograms, kernel density estimates, scatter plots of marginals)
 – Posterior predictive comparison plots (observed vs. simulated data)
 – Calibration curves (expected versus observed coverage for varying confidence levels)

──────────────────────────────
2. Class Structure and Interfaces

The Evaluation class is defined with public functions that our design requires. For example:

• __init__(model: Model, data: dict, config: dict)
 – Stores references to the trained model, evaluation dataset, and configuration parameters.  
 – Validates that the evaluation dataset contains both “observations” and “ground_truth” samples (or posteriors) for the desired conditionals.

• evaluate() -> dict
 – Main method that orchestrates metric computations and visualization generation.  
 – It will:
  a. Use the model’s forward method (and reverse diffusion via Euler–Maruyama, with steps given by config.evaluation.reverse_sde_steps) to produce inferred samples for different conditional distributions (e.g. posterior, likelihood).  
  b. Compare these inferred samples with corresponding reference (ground truth) samples from the evaluation data.
  c. Compute the C2ST metric by training a simple classifier (such as a logistic regression) that distinguishes between model samples and ground truth samples. The metric is the classifier’s accuracy; if ~0.5 then the model and reference distributions are nearly indistinguishable.
  d. Compute the Negative Log Likelihood using a probability flow ODE solver provided by our diffusion framework (this leverages the score model in our Model).  
  e. Conduct calibration analysis by performing an expected coverage computation: for a series of quantile levels (e.g., 10% up to 90%), determine if the ground truth lies within the model’s computed highest density region for that level and aggregate these results.
  f. Generate and possibly save plots visualizing marginal distributions, posterior predictives and calibration curves using matplotlib.
 – Returns a dictionary containing all metrics (e.g. { "c2st": value, "nll": value, "calibration": calibration_data, ... }).

• Helper functions (either as methods or private functions):
 – _compute_c2st(pred_samples, true_samples)  
  • Combines the two sample sets, labels them (e.g., 0 for ground truth, 1 for generated), splits data into training and validation sets, trains a simple classifier, and returns the accuracy (expected value near 0.5 for a good match).
 – _compute_nll(samples)  
  • Runs the probability flow ODE (using the diffusion module’s routine) on evaluation samples with the given reverse diffusion steps from config.  
  • Computes the per-sample log likelihood and returns the average NLL.
 – _compute_calibration(pred_samples, true_params)  
  • For each quantile level, checks whether the true parameter values lie within the corresponding highest-density region of the predicted posterior.
  • Aggregates these results and optionally produces a calibration curve.
 – _plot_posteriors(pred_samples, true_samples)  
  • Generates plots that overlay histograms or density estimates of the inferred and reference distributions.
 – _plot_predictives(predictive_samples, observations)  
  • Generates plots comparing posterior predictive distributions (e.g. simulated output data) with actual observed data.
 – _plot_calibration(calibration_data)  
  • Plots the calibration (expected vs. observed coverage) curve.

──────────────────────────────
3. Evaluation Workflow

Within the evaluate() method, the following steps will be carried out:

A. Data Preparation and Sample Generation  
 1. Retrieve the evaluation dataset dictionary. It should include keys such as 'parameters', 'observations', and reference samples for key conditionals (e.g. “ground_truth_posteriors”).  
 2. For each relevant conditional (e.g. posterior estimation), prepare the required condition mask (M_C) and any accompanying dynamic dependency masks (M_E) necessary to query the model.
 3. Use the model’s sampling/inference routine (via model.forward or an equivalent reverse diffusion process) to generate samples at t = 0 from an initial noise distribution. The reverse diffusion will be performed with a number of steps set by config.evaluation.reverse_sde_steps.

B. Metric Computation

 1. C2ST  
  – Call _compute_c2st(pred_samples, true_samples) for the relevant set of samples.  
  – Report the accuracy score; a value near 0.5 indicates that the inferred and reference distributions are nearly indistinguishable.

 2. Negative Log Likelihood (NLL)  
  – Call _compute_nll(samples) on the appropriate test set.  
  – This routine will invoke a numerical ODE solver to compute log likelihoods, then average them over samples.

 3. Calibration Analysis  
  – Call _compute_calibration(pred_samples, true_parameters) to compute expected coverage across a series of quantiles.  
  – This may involve computing a cumulative distribution function (CDF) or highest density regions from the predicted scores and checking if the ground truth lies within.

C. Visualization Generation

 1. Generate posterior density plots using _plot_posteriors(), comparing inferred samples vs. ground truth.  
 2. Generate posterior predictive plots using _plot_predictives(), where simulated outputs from the model are overlaid against the observed data.  
 3. Generate a calibration plot (expected vs. observed coverage) using _plot_calibration().

D. Aggregation and Output  
 – Organize all computed metrics and, if applicable, paths to saved plots into a dictionary and return/save this information for later inspection and logging.

──────────────────────────────
4. Configuration and Parameter Handling

• All hyperparameters relevant to evaluation are read from the config. For example:
 – config.evaluation.reverse_sde_steps (default: 500)
 – config.evaluation.c2st_threshold (expected 0.5 for a good match)
 – Other settings (e.g., whether to conduct NLL computation or calibration analysis) should be toggled via the configuration file.
• Use these configuration values strictly without hardcoding values elsewhere to facilitate reproducibility.

──────────────────────────────
5. Error Handling and Logging

• Validate that the evaluation dataset contains necessary keys and that the shapes of predicted samples coincide with those of the ground truth.  
• Log warnings if any evaluation metric cannot be computed due to missing data.  
• Optionally include logging statements to report intermediate values (e.g., “C2ST score computed: 0.52”, “Average NLL: –X.Y”, etc.) during execution.

──────────────────────────────
6. Integration with the Overall System

• The Evaluation module will be called by main.py after the Trainer has completed training.  
• It uses the Model’s forward or compute_score method (without altering the design) and the dataset provided by DatasetLoader.  
• Its design respects the pre-defined interfaces in our class diagram, ensuring modularity and consistency.

──────────────────────────────
Summary

The Evaluation module aims to quantitatively and qualitatively assess the Simformer’s performance. It computes key metrics—C2ST, NLL via the probability flow ODE, and calibration via expected coverage—and generates diagnostic plots to compare inferred distributions against reference posteriors. All computations strictly use configuration parameters (from config.yaml) and follow a modular design that integrates seamlessly with the Model and DatasetLoader components.

This thorough analysis will guide the coding of evaluation.py and ensure that all logic follows the methodology described in the paper while honoring the provided design and configuration. 67%|██████▋   | 4/6 [03:51<01:54, 57.11s/it]

[ANALYSIS] main.py
============================================
Below is a detailed logic analysis for main.py. This file serves as the entry point of the application and is responsible for configuration management (using Hydra), orchestrating the experiment’s flow, and ensuring that each module (data loading, model building, training, and evaluation) is called in the proper order. The logic analysis is written in a manner that aligns with the paper’s methodology, the provided plan, and the design details, while strictly following the configuration settings as specified in config.yaml.

──────────────────────────────
1. INITIALIZATION AND CONFIGURATION MANAGEMENT

• Use Hydra to load the global configuration from config.yaml. This configuration includes training hyperparameters (batch size, learning rate, max_epochs, etc.), SDE parameters (such as sigma_max, sigma_min, beta_min, and beta_max), diffusion reverse steps, model hyperparameters (token_dim, num_layers, num_heads, etc.), simulation settings (simulation_budget, condition mask types and random mask probabilities), and evaluation settings.

• Set random seeds (for both JAX and NumPy if needed) for reproducibility. This ensures that every run—with the given simulation budget and training setup—produces consistent results.

──────────────────────────────
2. DATA LOADING

• Instantiate the DatasetLoader class by passing the loaded config. The DatasetLoader is responsible for simulating and returning structured data for all benchmark tasks and domain-specific simulators (e.g., Gaussian Linear, Two Moons, Lotka–Volterra, SIRD, Hodgkin–Huxley, etc.).

• Call the load_data() method from DatasetLoader. This method should:
 - Generate (θ, x) simulation pairs.
 - Tokenize the data (using the defined tokenization procedure: each variable becomes a token with a unique identifier, its numeric value, and a binary condition flag).
 - Provide the condition masks (M_C) along with the corresponding dependency masks (M_E) as part of the data structure, ensuring consistency for training and later evaluation.

• The output is expected to be a structured dictionary (or similar data structure) containing keys such as 'parameters', 'observations', and 'condition_masks' among others.

──────────────────────────────
3. MODEL INITIALIZATION

• Create an instance of the Model class and pass in the model parameters from the configuration:
 - The transformer’s token dimension, the number of layers (6 by default – note: for tasks like Lotka–Volterra, SIRD, and Hodgkin–Huxley, this may be increased to 8), the number of attention heads (4), attention head size (10), feed-forward widening factor (3), and diffusion time embedding dimension (128).
 - The Model must also be designed to implement the forward(x, t, mask) method as well as compute_score(x, t, mask) which calculates the predicted scores based on the transformer architecture, tokenization details, and incorporates the dynamic attention masks. In these methods, the transformer must use both M_C (indicating which tokens are observed and should remain unchanged during noise injection) and M_E (representing dependency structures).

• The transformation of diffusion time using a random Fourier embedding (of dimension 128) is handled here in the model. This embedding will be combined with the token representations to form the complete input to the transformer.

──────────────────────────────
4. TRAINING PROCEDURE

• With the Model and the simulation data at hand, initialize the Trainer by providing the Model instance, the loaded dataset, and the configuration settings.

• The Trainer’s train() method is responsible for:
 - Handling mini-batch sampling (using the batch size defined in the config; in this case 1000).
 - For each training iteration:
  ○ Sample a time t uniformly from the SDE interval (e.g., [1e-5, 1.0]).
  ○ Generate noise to perform the forward SDE step, applying noise only to the unobserved components. This is done by computing x_MCₜ = (1 – M_C) ⊙ xₜ + M_C ⊙ x₀, so that observed tokens remain “clean.”
  ○ Call model.forward(batch_x, t, mask) to compute the predicted scores.
  ○ Compute the denoising score matching loss over the unobserved tokens, using the analytic target ∇ₓₜ log pₜ(xₜ|x₀) and only summing over components where M_C indicates unobserved data.
  ○ Update the model parameters using an Adam optimizer from Optax, with the learning rate from the configuration (1e-3).
 - Implement early stopping if validation loss does not improve and train for a maximum number of epochs (max_epochs is set to 100).
 - Log training progress and any other relevant metrics.

• The Trainer module thereby ensures that the diffusion-based learning strategy (with the SDE type, here “VESDE” based on the config) is followed closely, with all condition masks (joint, posterior, likelihood, or random) being sampled according to the probabilities defined (e.g., a Bernoulli draw with probability 0.3 for random masks).

──────────────────────────────
5. EVALUATION PROCEDURE

• Upon completing training, instantiate the Evaluation class. Pass the trained Model, the evaluation subset of the simulation data (or the entire dataset if required), and the configuration settings.

• The Evaluation.evaluate() method will:
 - Use the trained model to generate samples via the reverse diffusion process. This process uses Euler–Maruyama discretization with reverse_steps set to 500 (from the evaluation config).
 - For a given conditioned observation (or a set of observations/constraints), hold the observed tokens fixed and allow the model to sample the unobserved tokens.
 - Calculate the Classifier Two-Sample Test (C2ST) metric by comparing the model’s posterior (or arbitrary conditional) samples against reference samples obtained from an MCMC or HMC routine.
 - Optionally compute negative log likelihood (NLL) using the probability flow ODE, if nll_computation is set to true.
 - Run calibration analysis (expected coverage plots) to determine whether the true parameter lies within the indicated high-density regions as expected.
 - Generate and save qualitative visualizations (e.g., marginal posterior plots, posterior predictive plots) using matplotlib.

• Finally, Evaluation returns a dictionary of metrics and, optionally, saves plots for later inspection.

──────────────────────────────
6. ORCHESTRATION & FINALIZATION

• The main.py script will follow this flow:
 1. Load configuration via Hydra.
 2. Set up random seeds for reproducibility.
 3. Instantiate DatasetLoader and call load_data() to generate and preprocess simulation data.
 4. Instantiate the Model with model-specific hyperparameters from the config.
 5. Instantiate Trainer with the Model, dataset, and training parameters, then call train() to perform training.
 6. After training completes, instantiate Evaluation with the Model and dataset, and call evaluate() to obtain metrics and visualizations.
 7. Print or log the final evaluation metrics.
 8. (Optionally) Save model checkpoints and evaluation outputs.

• Throughout, every module receives configuration parameters from the same Hydra-loaded config.yaml ensuring consistency. No “magic numbers” will be hard-coded; instead, every hyperparameter (from transformer dimensions to SDE noise schedules) follows the configuration.

──────────────────────────────
7. CONCLUSION

The main.py file acts as the orchestrator for the entire experimental pipeline. It makes sure that:
 – Data is correctly simulated and prepared with the necessary tokens and masks.
 – The transformer-based score model is built with the right architecture.
 – The training loop accurately implements the denoising score matching loss and the forward SDE noise injection.
 – Evaluation comprehensively assesses the model using C2ST, NLL, and calibration metrics.
 
This logic is designed to mirror the methodologies presented in the paper while strictly adhering to configuration settings from config.yaml and the defined design of data structures and interfaces.

This analysis ensures that the final code implementation in main.py will follow the correct sequence, manage configurations and random seeding, and correctly tie together the dataset, model, trainer, and evaluation modules in a modular and extensible fashion. 83%|████████▎ | 5/6 [05:28<01:11, 71.47s/it]100%|██████████| 6/6 [05:28<00:00, 54.80s/it]

  0%|          | 0/6 [00:00<?, ?it/s][CODING] dataset_loader.py
============================================
```python
"""dataset_loader.py

This module implements the DatasetLoader class which is responsible for generating
simulation datasets for various tasks and packaging the data along with corresponding
conditioning masks.

Supported simulation tasks include:
    - "gaussian_linear"
    - "gaussian_mixture"
    - "two_moons"
    - "slcp"
    - "tree"
    - "hmm"
    - "lotka_volterra"
    - "sird"
    - "hodgkin_huxley"

The output of the load_data() method is a dictionary with keys:
    "parameters", "observations", and "condition_masks".

The condition_masks dictionary contains the following keys:
    "joint", "posterior", "likelihood", and "random".
    
Each condition mask is a binary array of shape (n_samples, total_tokens) where
total_tokens = (number of parameter tokens + number of observation tokens).

Configuration parameters (simulation budget, condition mask types, random mask probability)
are obtained from the provided config dictionary (typically loaded from config.yaml).
"""

import numpy as np
import jax.numpy as jnp  # imported for potential JAX operations if needed


class DatasetLoader:
    def __init__(self, config: dict):
        """
        Initialize the DatasetLoader.

        Args:
            config (dict): Configuration dictionary loaded via Hydra.
                           Expected keys:
                               - simulation.simulation_budget (int)
                               - simulation.condition_mask.types (list of str)
                               - simulation.condition_mask.random_mask_probability (float)
        """
        self.config = config
        # Set default simulation budget if not provided
        self.simulation_budget = config.get("simulation", {}).get("simulation_budget", 100000)
        # Read condition mask types; default is all four mask types.
        self.mask_types = config.get("simulation", {}).get("condition_mask", {}).get(
            "types", ["joint", "posterior", "likelihood", "random"]
        )
        # Random mask probability for Bernoulli sampling; default 0.3.
        self.random_mask_prob = config.get("simulation", {}).get("condition_mask", {}).get(
            "random_mask_probability", 0.3
        )

    def load_data(self, task: str = "gaussian_linear") -> dict:
        """
        Generate simulation data and corresponding condition masks for the specified task.

        Args:
            task (str): Simulation task identifier. Supported values are:
                        "gaussian_linear", "gaussian_mixture", "two_moons",
                        "slcp", "tree", "hmm", "lotka_volterra", "sird", "hodgkin_huxley".
                        Defaults to "gaussian_linear".

        Returns:
            dict: A dictionary with keys:
                  "parameters": np.ndarray of shape (n_samples, d_parameters)
                  "observations": np.ndarray of shape (n_samples, d_observations)
                  "condition_masks": dict with keys "joint", "posterior", "likelihood", "random"
                                    Each mask has shape (n_samples, d_parameters + d_observations).
        """
        supported_tasks = {
            "gaussian_linear",
            "gaussian_mixture",
            "two_moons",
            "slcp",
            "tree",
            "hmm",
            "lotka_volterra",
            "sird",
            "hodgkin_huxley",
        }
        if task not in supported_tasks:
            raise ValueError(f"Unsupported simulation task: {task}")

        # Select simulation routine based on task.
        if task == "gaussian_linear":
            parameters, observations = self._simulate_gaussian_linear()
        elif task == "gaussian_mixture":
            parameters, observations = self._simulate_gaussian_mixture()
        elif task == "two_moons":
            parameters, observations = self._simulate_two_moons()
        elif task == "slcp":
            parameters, observations = self._simulate_slcp()
        elif task == "tree":
            parameters, observations = self._simulate_tree()
        elif task == "hmm":
            parameters, observations = self._simulate_hmm()
        elif task == "lotka_volterra":
            parameters, observations = self._simulate_lotka_volterra()
        elif task == "sird":
            parameters, observations = self._simulate_sird()
        elif task == "hodgkin_huxley":
            parameters, observations = self._simulate_hodgkin_huxley()
        else:
            # Not reachable due to earlier check
            raise ValueError(f"Task {task} is not implemented.")

        # Determine dimensions.
        n_samples = parameters.shape[0]
        d_params = parameters.shape[1]
        d_obs = observations.shape[1]
        total_tokens = d_params + d_obs

        # Generate condition masks.
        condition_masks = {}
        for mask_type in self.mask_types:
            if mask_type == "joint":
                # Joint mask makes all tokens unobserved (0 means unobserved).
                mask = np.zeros((n_samples, total_tokens), dtype=np.int32)
            elif mask_type == "posterior":
                # Posterior: parameters are unobserved (0), observations are observed (1).
                mask_params = np.zeros((n_samples, d_params), dtype=np.int32)
                mask_obs = np.ones((n_samples, d_obs), dtype=np.int32)
                mask = np.concatenate([mask_params, mask_obs], axis=1)
            elif mask_type == "likelihood":
                # Likelihood: parameters are observed (1), observations are unobserved (0).
                mask_params = np.ones((n_samples, d_params), dtype=np.int32)
                mask_obs = np.zeros((n_samples, d_obs), dtype=np.int32)
                mask = np.concatenate([mask_params, mask_obs], axis=1)
            elif mask_type == "random":
                # Random mask: each token is observed (1) with probability random_mask_prob.
                mask = np.random.binomial(1, self.random_mask_prob, size=(n_samples, total_tokens)).astype(np.int32)
            else:
                raise ValueError(f"Unsupported mask type: {mask_type}")
            condition_masks[mask_type] = mask

        # Package the simulation data and condition masks into a dictionary.
        data = {
            "parameters": parameters,
            "observations": observations,
            "condition_masks": condition_masks,
        }
        return data

    def _simulate_gaussian_linear(self) -> tuple:
        """
        Simulate the Gaussian Linear task.
        Parameters (θ) are sampled from N(0, 0.1 I) with dimension 10.
        Observations (x) are sampled as x = θ + ε with ε ~ N(0, 0.1 I).

        Returns:
            tuple: (parameters, observations) as numpy arrays.
                   parameters has shape (n_samples, 10)
                   observations has shape (n_samples, 10)
        """
        n = self.simulation_budget
        d = 10
        std_param = np.sqrt(0.1)
        parameters = np.random.normal(0, std_param, size=(n, d))
        observations = parameters + np.random.normal(0, std_param, size=(n, d))
        return parameters, observations

    def _simulate_gaussian_mixture(self) -> tuple:
        """
        Simulate the Gaussian Mixture task.
        Parameters (θ) are sampled from Uniform(-10, 10) in ℝ^2.
        Observations (x) are generated from a mixture:
            With probability 0.5, x ~ N(θ, I)
            With probability 0.5, x ~ N(θ, 0.01 I)

        Returns:
            tuple: (parameters, observations) as numpy arrays.
                   parameters has shape (n_samples, 2)
                   observations has shape (n_samples, 2)
        """
        n = self.simulation_budget
        d = 2
        parameters = np.random.uniform(-10, 10, size=(n, d))
        observations = np.zeros((n, d))
        for i in range(n):
            if np.random.rand() < 0.5:
                std = 1.0  # Standard deviation for covariance I.
            else:
                std = 0.1  # Standard deviation for covariance 0.01 I.
            observations[i] = parameters[i] + np.random.normal(0, std, size=d)
        return parameters, observations

    def _simulate_two_moons(self) -> tuple:
        """
        Simulate the Two Moons task.
        Parameters (θ) are sampled from Uniform(-1, 1) in ℝ^1.
        Observations (x) in ℝ^2 are generated as:
            x1 = cos(pi * θ) + noise, x2 = sin(pi * θ) + noise.

        Returns:
            tuple: (parameters, observations)
                   parameters has shape (n_samples, 1)
                   observations has shape (n_samples, 2)
        """
        n = self.simulation_budget
        d_param = 1
        d_obs = 2
        parameters = np.random.uniform(-1, 1, size=(n, d_param))
        noise_std = 0.1
        x1 = np.cos(np.pi * parameters[:, 0]) + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, size=n)
        x2 = np.sin(np.pi * parameters[:, 0]) + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, size=n)
        observations = np.stack([x1, x2], axis=1)
        return parameters, observations

    def _simulate_slcp(self) -> tuple:
        """
        Simulate the SLCP (Simple Likelihood Complex Posterior) task.
        Parameters (θ) are sampled from Uniform(-3, 3) in ℝ^1.
        Observations (x) in ℝ^4 are generated with nonlinear relationships.

        Returns:
            tuple: (parameters, observations)
                   parameters has shape (n_samples, 1)
                   observations has shape (n_samples, 4)
        """
        n = self.simulation_budget
        d_param = 1
        d_obs = 4
        parameters = np.random.uniform(-3, 3, size=(n, d_param))
        noise_std = 0.1
        x0 = parameters[:, 0] + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, size=n)
        x1 = (parameters[:, 0] ** 2) + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, size=n)
        x2 = np.sin(parameters[:, 0]) + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, size=n)
        x3 = np.cos(parameters[:, 0]) + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, size=n)
        observations = np.stack([x0, x1, x2, x3], axis=1)
        return parameters, observations

    def _simulate_tree(self) -> tuple:
        """
        Simulate the Tree task with a tree-structured dependency.
        Parameters (θ) are generated as:
            θ0 ~ N(0,1), θ1 ~ N(θ0,1), θ2 ~ N(θ0,1) in ℝ^3.
        Observations (x) in ℝ^4 are generated as:
            x0 = sin(θ1)^2 + noise, x1 = 0.1*θ2 + noise,
            x2 = 0.1*θ2 + noise, x3 = cos(θ2)^2 + noise.

        Returns:
            tuple: (parameters, observations)
                   parameters has shape (n_samples, 3)
                   observations has shape (n_samples, 4)
        """
        n = self.simulation_budget
        theta0 = np.random.normal(0, 1, size=(n, 1))
        theta1 = theta0 + np.random.normal(0, 1, size=(n, 1))
        theta2 = theta0 + np.random.normal(0, 1, size=(n, 1))
        parameters = np.concatenate([theta0, theta1, theta2], axis=1)
        noise_std_0 = 0.2
        noise_std_1 = 0.2
        noise_std_2 = 0.6
        noise_std_3 = 0.1
        x0 = np.sin(theta1[:, 0]) ** 2 + np.random.normal(0, noise_std_0, size=n)
        x1 = 0.1 * theta2[:, 0] + np.random.normal(0, noise_std_1, size=n)
        x2 = 0.1 * theta2[:, 0] + np.random.normal(0, noise_std_2, size=n)
        x3 = np.cos(theta2[:, 0]) ** 2 + np.random.normal(0, noise_std_3, size=n)
        observations = np.stack([x0, x1, x2, x3], axis=1)
        return parameters, observations

    def _simulate_hmm(self) -> tuple:
        """
        Simulate the Hidden Markov Model (HMM) task.
        Parameters (θ) are generated as an autoregressive sequence in ℝ^10:
            θ[0] ~ N(0, 0.5), θ[i] ~ N(θ[i-1], 0.5) for i = 1,...,9.
        Observations (x) are generated as:
            x[i] = θ[i]^2 + noise with noise std 0.5, for i = 0,...,9.

        Returns:
            tuple: (parameters, observations)
                   parameters has shape (n_samples, 10)
                   observations has shape (n_samples, 10)
        """
        n = self.simulation_budget
        d = 10
        parameters = np.zeros((n, d))
        observations = np.zeros((n, d))
        std_initial = 0.5
        std_transition = 0.5
        std_obs = 0.5
        for i in range(n):
            theta = np.zeros(d)
            x = np.zeros(d)
            theta[0] = np.random.normal(0, std_initial)
            x[0] = theta[0] ** 2 + np.random.normal(0, std_obs)
            for j in range(1, d):
                theta[j] = np.random.normal(theta[j - 1], std_transition)
                x[j] = theta[j] ** 2 + np.random.normal(0, std_obs)
            parameters[i] = theta
            observations[i] = x
        return parameters, observations

    def _simulate_lotka_volterra(self) -> tuple:
        """
        Simulate the Lotka–Volterra (Ecology) task.
        Parameters (θ) are 4 values sampled uniformly from [1, 3].
        Observations (x) are generated by simulating the predator-prey ODE:
            dx/dt = α*x - β*x*y, dy/dt = δ*x*y - γ*y,
        with initial conditions x0 = 10, y0 = 5, simulated via Euler integration.
        Four observation time points for each species are sampled, yielding an observation
        vector in ℝ^8 (first 4 tokens for prey, next 4 tokens for predator).

        Returns:
            tuple: (parameters, observations)
                   parameters has shape (n_samples, 4)
                   observations has shape (n_samples, 8)
        """
        n = self.simulation_budget
        d_params = 4
        n_obs_points = 4  # number of observation time points per species
        d_obs = n_obs_points * 2  # prey and predator observations
        parameters = np.zeros((n, d_params))
        observations = np.zeros((n, d_obs))
        T = 20.0
        steps = 100
        dt = T / steps
        for i in range(n):
            # Sample parameters uniformly in [1, 3]
            alpha = np.random.uniform(1, 3)
            beta = np.random.uniform(1, 3)
            gamma = np.random.uniform(1, 3)
            delta = np.random.uniform(1, 3)
            parameters[i] = np.array([alpha, beta, gamma, delta])
            # Initialize populations for prey and predator.
            x = 10.0
            y = 5.0
            prey_series = []
            predator_series = []
            # Euler integration over time.
            for t in range(steps):
                dx = alpha * x - beta * x * y
                dy = delta * x * y - gamma * y
                x = x + dx * dt
                y = y + dy * dt
                # Sample observation at fixed intervals.
                if t % (steps // n_obs_points) == 0:
                    prey_series.append(x + np.random.normal(0, 0.1))
                    predator_series.append(y + np.random.normal(0, 0.1))
            # Adjust the length of the series if necessary.
            if len(prey_series) > n_obs_points:
                prey_series = prey_series[:n_obs_points]
                predator_series = predator_series[:n_obs_points]
            elif len(prey_series) < n_obs_points:
                while len(prey_series) < n_obs_points:
                    prey_series.append(prey_series[-1])
                    predator_series.append(predator_series[-1])
            observations[i] = np.concatenate([np.array(prey_series), np.array(predator_series)])
        return parameters, observations

    def _simulate_sird(self) -> tuple:
        """
        Simulate the SIRD (Epidemiology) task.
        Parameters (θ) are 3 values representing:
            recovery_rate (γ), death_rate (μ), and contact_rate (β).
        Observations (x) are generated by simulating the SIRD ODE:
            dS/dt = -β*S*I,
            dI/dt = β*S*I - γ*I - μ*I,
            dR/dt = γ*I,
            dD/dt = μ*I,
        with initial conditions: S = 1000, I = 10, R = 0, D = 0.
        Three observation time points for each of I, R, and D are sampled,
        yielding an observation vector in ℝ^9.

        Returns:
            tuple: (parameters, observations)
                   parameters has shape (n_samples, 3)
                   observations has shape (n_samples, 9)
        """
        n = self.simulation_budget
        d_params = 3
        n_obs_points = 3
        d_obs = n_obs_points * 3  # for I, R, D observations
        parameters = np.zeros((n, d_params))
        observations = np.zeros((n, d_obs))
        T = 50.0
        steps = 100
        dt = T / steps
        for i in range(n):
            gamma = np.random.uniform(0.0, 0.5)
            mu = np.random.uniform(0.0, 0.5)
            beta = np.random.uniform(0.0, 1.0)
            parameters[i] = np.array([gamma, mu, beta])
            S = 1000.0
            I = 10.0
            R = 0.0
            D = 0.0
            I_series = []
            R_series = []
            D_series = []
            for t in range(steps):
                dS = -beta * S * I
                dI = beta * S * I - gamma * I - mu * I
                dR = gamma * I
                dD = mu * I
                S = S + dS * dt
                I = I + dI * dt
                R = R + dR * dt
                D = D + dD * dt
                if t % (steps // n_obs_points) == 0:
                    I_series.append(I + np.random.normal(0, 0.05))
                    R_series.append(R + np.random.normal(0, 0.05))
                    D_series.append(D + np.random.normal(0, 0.05))
            if len(I_series) > n_obs_points:
                I_series = I_series[:n_obs_points]
                R_series = R_series[:n_obs_points]
                D_series = D_series[:n_obs_points]
            elif len(I_series) < n_obs_points:
                while len(I_series) < n_obs_points:
                    I_series.append(I_series[-1])
                    R_series.append(R_series[-1])
                    D_series.append(D_series[-1])
            observations[i] = np.concatenate([np.array(I_series), np.array(R_series), np.array(D_series)])
        return parameters, observations

    def _simulate_hodgkin_huxley(self) -> tuple:
        """
        Simulate the Hodgkin–Huxley (Neuroscience) task.
        Parameters (θ) are 7 values sampled from N(0,1).
        Observations (x) are dummy summary statistics calculated as:
            voltage_summary = tanh(sum(θ)) + noise,
            energy = |sum(θ)| + noise,
            variance = std(θ) + noise.
        This is a simplified placeholder simulation.

        Returns:
            tuple: (parameters, observations)
                   parameters has shape (n_samples, 7)
                   observations has shape (n_samples, 3)
        """
        n = self.simulation_budget
        d_params = 7
        d_obs = 3
        parameters = np.random.normal(0, 1, size=(n, d_params))
        noise_std = 0.1
        voltage_summary = np.tanh(np.sum(parameters, axis=1)) + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, size=n)
        energy = np.abs(np.sum(parameters, axis=1)) + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, size=n)
        variance = np.std(parameters, axis=1) + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, size=n)
        observations = np.stack([voltage_summary, energy, variance], axis=1)
        return parameters, observations


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # For standalone testing the DatasetLoader.
    import yaml

    try:
        with open("config.yaml", "r") as f:
            config = yaml.safe_load(f)
    except Exception as e:
        print("Error loading configuration:", e)
        config = {}

    # Instantiate the DatasetLoader with the configuration.
    loader = DatasetLoader(config)
    # Load data for the default task: gaussian_linear.
    data = loader.load_data(task="gaussian_linear")
    print("Data Keys:", list(data.keys()))
    print("Parameters shape:", data["parameters"].shape)
    print("Observations shape:", data["observations"].shape)
    print("Condition Masks Available:", list(data["condition_masks"].keys()))

[CODING] model.py

```python
"""model.py

This module implements the Model class which encapsulates the transformer‐based score model.
It performs tokenization with identifier and condition embeddings, encodes diffusion time using a
random Fourier embedding, and processes the tokens via multiple transformer blocks. Finally, it
projects the output to produce predicted scores matching the input token dimensions.
"""

import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax import random, nn
import numpy as np

# --------------------------
# Basic Dense layer
# --------------------------
class Dense:
    def __init__(self, key: jax.random.PRNGKey, input_dim: int, output_dim: int) -> None:
        self.input_dim: int = input_dim
        self.output_dim: int = output_dim
        k1, k2 = random.split(key)
        # Initialize weights with Xavier scaling
        self.w: jnp.ndarray = random.normal(k1, (input_dim, output_dim)) * jnp.sqrt(2.0 / (input_dim + output_dim))
        self.b: jnp.ndarray = jnp.zeros((output_dim,))

    def __call__(self, x: jnp.ndarray) -> jnp.ndarray:
        # x shape: (..., input_dim)
        return jnp.dot(x, self.w) + self.b


# --------------------------
# Layer Normalization
# --------------------------
class LayerNorm:
    def __init__(self, key: jax.random.PRNGKey, dim: int, eps: float = 1e-6) -> None:
        self.dim: int = dim
        self.eps: float = eps
        # Initialize scale and bias as trainable parameters.
        self.scale: jnp.ndarray = jnp.ones((dim,))
        self.bias: jnp.ndarray = jnp.zeros((dim,))

    def __call__(self, x: jnp.ndarray) -> jnp.ndarray:
        # Compute mean and variance over the last dimension.
        mean = jnp.mean(x, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
        var = jnp.mean((x - mean) ** 2, axis=-1, keepdims=True)
        return self.scale * (x - mean) / jnp.sqrt(var + self.eps) + self.bias


# --------------------------
# Multi-Head Self-Attention Layer
# --------------------------
class MultiHeadSelfAttention:
    def __init__(self, key: jax.random.PRNGKey, token_dim: int, num_heads: int, head_size: int) -> None:
        self.token_dim: int = token_dim
        self.num_heads: int = num_heads
        self.head_size: int = head_size
        self.total_head_dim: int = num_heads * head_size

        # Split key for weight initializations of Q, K, V and the output projection
        kq, kk, kv, ko = random.split(key, 4)
        self.wq = Dense(kq, token_dim, self.total_head_dim)
        self.wk = Dense(kk, token_dim, self.total_head_dim)
        self.wv = Dense(kv, token_dim, self.total_head_dim)
        self.wo = Dense(ko, self.total_head_dim, token_dim)

    def __call__(self, x: jnp.ndarray, attn_mask: jnp.ndarray = None) -> jnp.ndarray:
        """
        Args:
            x: jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, token_dim)
            attn_mask: Optional jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, seq_len). For masked positions, value 0.
        
        Returns:
            jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, token_dim)
        """
        batch_size, seq_len, _ = x.shape

        # Compute linear projections: Q, K, V
        q = self.wq(x)  # (batch, seq_len, total_head_dim)
        k = self.wk(x)
        v = self.wv(x)

        # Reshape and transpose to shape (batch, num_heads, seq_len, head_size)
        def reshape_for_heads(z: jnp.ndarray) -> jnp.ndarray:
            z = z.reshape(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_size)
            return jnp.transpose(z, (0, 2, 1, 3))
        
        q = reshape_for_heads(q)
        k = reshape_for_heads(k)
        v = reshape_for_heads(v)

        # Scaled dot-product attention.
        scale = 1.0 / jnp.sqrt(self.head_size)
        attn_logits = jnp.einsum('bnqd, bnkd -> bnqk', q, k) * scale  # shape (batch, num_heads, seq_len, seq_len)

        if attn_mask is not None:
            # attn_mask expected shape: (batch, seq_len, seq_len)
            # Expand to (batch, 1, seq_len, seq_len) if necessary.
            if attn_mask.ndim == 3:
                attn_mask = jnp.expand_dims(attn_mask, axis=1)
            # Where mask==0, add large negative value.
            attn_logits = attn_logits + (1.0 - attn_mask) * (-1e9)

        attn_weights = nn.softmax(attn_logits, axis=-1)
        attn_output = jnp.einsum('bnqk, bnkd -> bnqd', attn_weights, v)
        # Transpose and reshape back to (batch, seq_len, total_head_dim)
        attn_output = jnp.transpose(attn_output, (0, 2, 1, 3)).reshape(batch_size, seq_len, self.total_head_dim)
        # Final linear projection
        output = self.wo(attn_output)
        return output


# --------------------------
# Transformer Block
# --------------------------
class TransformerBlock:
    def __init__(self, key: jax.random.PRNGKey, token_dim: int, num_heads: int, head_size: int, ffn_hidden_dim: int) -> None:
        # Split keys for layer norm, attention, feed-forward layers
        keys = random.split(key, 4)
        self.ln1 = LayerNorm(keys[0], token_dim)
        self.attn = MultiHeadSelfAttention(keys[1], token_dim, num_heads, head_size)
        self.ln2 = LayerNorm(keys[2], token_dim)
        self.ffn_dense1 = Dense(keys[3], token_dim, ffn_hidden_dim)
        # Use another split for the second FFN dense layer.
        new_key = random.split(key)[0]
        self.ffn_dense2 = Dense(new_key, ffn_hidden_dim, token_dim)

    def __call__(self, x: jnp.ndarray, attn_mask: jnp.ndarray = None) -> jnp.ndarray:
        # Multi-head self-attention sub-layer with residual connection.
        h = self.ln1(x)
        attn_out = self.attn(h, attn_mask)
        x = x + attn_out

        # Feed-forward network sub-layer with residual connection.
        h_ffn = self.ln2(x)
        ffn_out = self.ffn_dense2(nn.relu(self.ffn_dense1(h_ffn)))
        x = x + ffn_out
        return x


# --------------------------
# Model Class: Transformer-based Score Model
# --------------------------
class Model:
    def __init__(self, config: dict) -> None:
        """
        Initializes the transformer-based score model.
        Loads configuration parameters from config.yaml via the config dictionary.
        """
        self.config = config
        model_config = config.get("model", {})

        # Configuration parameters
        self.token_dim: int = int(model_config.get("token_dim", 50))
        self.diffusion_time_embed_dim: int = int(model_config.get("diffusion_time_embed_dim", 128))
        self.num_layers: int = int(model_config.get("num_layers", 6))
        self.num_heads: int = int(model_config.get("num_heads", 4))
        self.attention_head_size: int = int(model_config.get("attention_head_size", 10))
        self.ffn_widening_factor: int = int(model_config.get("ffn_widening_factor", 3))
        self.ffn_hidden_dim: int = self.token_dim * self.ffn_widening_factor
        # Raw token dimension (e.g., 1 for scalar values)
        self.raw_value_dim: int = int(model_config.get("raw_value_dim", 1))
        # Number of parameter tokens (assumed to be the first tokens in the sequence)
        self.num_param_tokens: int = int(model_config.get("num_param_tokens", 10))

        # Initialize PRNG key
        self.rng_key = random.PRNGKey(0)

        # Input projection: From raw value to token embedding
        key, subkey = random.split(self.rng_key)
        self.input_proj = Dense(subkey, self.raw_value_dim, self.token_dim)

        # Identifier embeddings for tokens (parameters and observations)
        key, subkey = random.split(key)
        self.param_id_embed: jnp.ndarray = random.normal(subkey, (self.token_dim,))
        key, subkey = random.split(key)
        self.obs_id_embed: jnp.ndarray = random.normal(subkey, (self.token_dim,))

        # Condition flag embeddings: Two embeddings for binary condition (0 or 1)
        key, subkey = random.split(key)
        self.cond_embeds: jnp.ndarray = random.normal(subkey, (2, self.token_dim))

        # Random Fourier embedding for diffusion time.
        # We use half the final dimension for cos and half for sin.
        key, subkey = random.split(key)
        rfe_dim: int = self.diffusion_time_embed_dim // 2
        self.fourier_weights: jnp.ndarray = random.normal(subkey, (rfe_dim,))
        key, subkey = random.split(key)
        self.fourier_bias: jnp.ndarray = random.uniform(subkey, (rfe_dim,), minval=0.0, maxval=2 * jnp.pi)

        # Dense layer to project Fourier time embedding to token_dim.
        key, subkey = random.split(key)
        self.time_dense = Dense(subkey, self.diffusion_time_embed_dim, self.token_dim)

        # Build transformer blocks.
        self.transformer_blocks = []
        for i in range(self.num_layers):
            key, block_key = random.split(key)
            block = TransformerBlock(block_key, self.token_dim, self.num_heads, self.attention_head_size, self.ffn_hidden_dim)
            self.transformer_blocks.append(block)

        # Final projection: From token_dim back to raw value dimension (score dimension).
        key, subkey = random.split(key)
        self.output_proj = Dense(subkey, self.token_dim, self.raw_value_dim)

    def _compute_time_embedding(self, t: jnp.ndarray) -> jnp.ndarray:
        """
        Compute a random Fourier embedding for the diffusion time t.
        
        Args:
            t: A scalar or a jnp.ndarray of shape (batch,) representing diffusion time(s).

        Returns:
            A jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, diffusion_time_embed_dim) (or (diffusion_time_embed_dim,) for a scalar).
        """
        t = jnp.atleast_1d(t)  # Ensure t is at least 1D, shape (batch,) if scalar was provided.
        # Expand dims for broadcasting: shape (batch, 1)
        t_expanded = t[:, None]
        # Compute the projection: (batch, rfe_dim)
        projection = t_expanded * self.fourier_weights[None, :] + self.fourier_bias[None, :]
        # Concatenate cos and sin features to obtain full diffusion_time_embed_dim.
        cos_emb = jnp.cos(projection)
        sin_emb = jnp.sin(projection)
        time_emb = jnp.concatenate([cos_emb, sin_emb], axis=-1)
        return time_emb  # shape: (batch, diffusion_time_embed_dim)

    def _add_identifier_and_condition_embeddings(
        self, x: jnp.ndarray, cond_mask: jnp.ndarray
    ) -> jnp.ndarray:
        """
        Add the identifier embedding (based on token type) and condition flag embedding to x.
        
        Args:
            x: jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, token_dim) representing token value embeddings.
            cond_mask: jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len) with binary values (0 for unobserved, 1 for observed).
        
        Returns:
            jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, token_dim) with added embeddings.
        """
        batch_size, seq_len, _ = x.shape
        # Create token type array: first num_param_tokens are parameters (type 0), rest are observations (type 1).
        token_types = jnp.concatenate([
            jnp.zeros(self.num_param_tokens, dtype=jnp.int32),
            jnp.ones(seq_len - self.num_param_tokens, dtype=jnp.int32)
        ], axis=0)
        # Broadcast token_types to shape (batch, seq_len)
        token_types = jnp.broadcast_to(token_types, (batch_size, seq_len))
        # Lookup identifier embeddings for each token.
        param_embed = jnp.broadcast_to(self.param_id_embed, (batch_size, seq_len, self.token_dim))
        obs_embed = jnp.broadcast_to(self.obs_id_embed, (batch_size, seq_len, self.token_dim))
        identifier_embed = jnp.where(token_types[..., None] == 0, param_embed, obs_embed)
        # Lookup condition embeddings: cond_mask values (0 or 1) used as indices.
        cond_embed = jnp.take(self.cond_embeds, cond_mask, axis=0)
        return x + identifier_embed + cond_embed

    def forward(self, x: jnp.ndarray, t: float, mask: dict) -> jnp.ndarray:
        """
        Forward pass through the transformer-based score model.
        
        Args:
            x: jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, raw_value_dim) representing raw token values.
            t: A scalar or a jnp.ndarray representing the diffusion time.
            mask: A dictionary with keys:
                "cond": jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len) containing the condition mask (observed tokens as 1, unobserved as 0).
                "dep": Optional jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, seq_len) representing the dependency mask for attention.
        
        Returns:
            jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, raw_value_dim) representing the predicted scores.
        """
        batch_size, seq_len, _ = x.shape

        # 1. Token Embedding: Project raw token values to token_dim.
        x_emb = self.input_proj(x)  # shape: (batch, seq_len, token_dim)

        # 2. Add Identifier and Condition Embeddings.
        # Expecting mask dictionary with key "cond". Default to all zeros if not provided.
        cond_mask = mask.get("cond", jnp.zeros((batch_size, seq_len), dtype=jnp.int32))
        x_emb = self._add_identifier_and_condition_embeddings(x_emb, cond_mask)

        # 3. Diffusion Time Embedding.
        time_emb = self._compute_time_embedding(t)  # shape: (batch, diffusion_time_embed_dim)
        # If necessary, broadcast to batch size.
        if time_emb.shape[0] != batch_size:
            time_emb = jnp.broadcast_to(time_emb, (batch_size, time_emb.shape[1]))
        # Project time embedding to token_dim.
        time_proj = self.time_dense(time_emb)  # shape: (batch, token_dim)
        # Expand and broadcast time_proj to (batch, seq_len, token_dim)
        time_proj = jnp.expand_dims(time_proj, axis=1)
        time_proj = jnp.broadcast_to(time_proj, (batch_size, seq_len, self.token_dim))
        # Add the time projection to token embeddings.
        x_emb = x_emb + time_proj

        # 4. Transformer Processing.
        # Fetch dependency mask from dictionary; key "dep" is expected.
        dep_mask = mask.get("dep", None)  # shape: (batch, seq_len, seq_len) if provided
        for transformer in self.transformer_blocks:
            x_emb = transformer(x_emb, attn_mask=dep_mask)

        # 5. Final Projection to raw_value_dim (score estimation).
        out = self.output_proj(x_emb)  # shape: (batch, seq_len, raw_value_dim)
        return out

    def compute_score(self, x: jnp.ndarray, t: float, mask: dict) -> jnp.ndarray:
        """
        Compute the score (estimate of ∇ₓ log pₜ(x)) by wrapping the forward pass.
        
        Args:
            x: jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, raw_value_dim)
            t: A scalar or jnp.ndarray representing diffusion time.
            mask: A dictionary with keys "cond" and optionally "dep".
        
        Returns:
            jnp.ndarray of shape (batch, seq_len, raw_value_dim) representing predicted scores.
        """
        return self.forward(x, t, mask)

```python
"""trainer.py

This module implements the Trainer class which orchestrates the full training loop for the
Simformer model. It samples mini-batches from simulated datasets, injects forward noise via an
SDE (here using the Variance Exploding SDE), computes the denoising score matching loss, and
updates the model parameters using Optax’s Adam optimizer.

The Trainer assumes that the model is a transformer‐based score model (as defined in model.py)
and that the dataset is provided by DatasetLoader (from dataset_loader.py). Configuration
parameters (training hyperparameters, SDE settings, simulation settings, and condition mask
specifications) are loaded from config.yaml.
"""

import time
import numpy as np
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax import random
import optax

# --------------------------
# Trainer Class
# --------------------------
class Trainer:
    def __init__(self, model, data: dict, config: dict) -> None:
        """
        Initialize the Trainer.

        Args:
            model: An instance of the transformer-based score model.
                   Must implement forward(x, t, mask) and compute_score(x, t, mask).
            data (dict): Dictionary containing simulation samples with keys:
                         "parameters", "observations", and "condition_masks".
            config (dict): Configuration dictionary loaded from config.yaml.
                           Expected keys include "training", "sde", and "simulation".
        """
        self.model = model
        self.data = data
        self.config = config

        # Training hyperparameters
        self.batch_size: int = int(config.get("training", {}).get("batch_size", 1000))
        self.learning_rate: float = float(config.get("training", {}).get("learning_rate", 1e-3))
        self.max_epochs: int = int(config.get("training", {}).get("max_epochs", 100))
        self.early_stopping: bool = bool(config.get("training", {}).get("early_stopping", True))

        # SDE parameters (using VESDE as default)
        sde_config = config.get("sde", {})
        self.sde_type: str = sde_config.get("type", "VESDE")
        self.sigma_max: float = float(sde_config.get("sigma_max", 15.0))
        self.sigma_min: float = float(sde_config.get("sigma_min", 0.0001))
        self.beta_min: float = float(sde_config.get("beta_min", 0.01))
        self.beta_max: float = float(sde_config.get("beta_max", 10.0))

        # PRNG key for randomness (used for noise sampling, mask sampling, etc.)
        self.rng = random.PRNGKey(42)

        # Dataset size and token count
        self.n_samples: int = self.data["parameters"].shape[0]
        d_params = self.data["parameters"].shape[1]
        d_obs = self.data["observations"].shape[1]
        self.num_tokens: int = d_params + d_obs

        # Extract initial model parameters from the model.
        self.params: dict = self.get_params()

        # Set up the optimizer.
        self.optimizer = optax.adam(self.learning_rate)
        self.opt_state = self.optimizer.init(self.params)

    def get_params(self) -> dict:
        """
        Extracts the trainable parameters from the model into a nested dictionary.
        Returns:
            dict: A dictionary of model parameters.
        """
        params = {
            "input_proj": {"w": self.model.input_proj.w, "b": self.model.input_proj.b},
            "param_id_embed": self.model.param_id_embed,
            "obs_id_embed": self.model.obs_id_embed,
            "cond_embeds": self.model.cond_embeds,
            "fourier_weights": self.model.fourier_weights,
            "fourier_bias": self.model.fourier_bias,
            "time_dense": {"w": self.model.time_dense.w, "b": self.model.time_dense.b},
            "transformer_blocks": [
                {
                    "ln1": {"scale": block.ln1.scale, "bias": block.ln1.bias},
                    "attn": {
                        "wq": {"w": block.attn.wq.w, "b": block.attn.wq.b},
                        "wk": {"w": block.attn.wk.w, "b": block.attn.wk.b},
                        "wv": {"w": block.attn.wv.w, "b": block.attn.wv.b},
                        "wo": {"w": block.attn.wo.w, "b": block.attn.wo.b},
                    },
                    "ln2": {"scale": block.ln2.scale, "bias": block.ln2.bias},
                    "ffn_dense1": {"w": block.ffn_dense1.w, "b": block.ffn_dense1.b},
                    "ffn_dense2": {"w": block.ffn_dense2.w, "b": block.ffn_dense2.b},
                }
                for block in self.model.transformer_blocks
            ],
            "output_proj": {"w": self.model.output_proj.w, "b": self.model.output_proj.b},
        }
        return params

    def set_params(self, params: dict) -> None:
        """
        Updates the model's trainable parameters using the provided parameter dictionary.
        
        Args:
            params (dict): A nested dictionary of model parameters.
        """
        self.model.input_proj.w = params["input_proj"]["w"]
        self.model.input_proj.b = params["input_proj"]["b"]
        self.model.param_id_embed = params["param_id_embed"]
        self.model.obs_id_embed = params["obs_id_embed"]
        self.model.cond_embeds = params["cond_embeds"]
        self.model.fourier_weights = params["fourier_weights"]
        self.model.fourier_bias = params["fourier_bias"]
        self.model.time_dense.w = params["time_dense"]["w"]
        self.model.time_dense.b = params["time_dense"]["b"]
        for i, block in enumerate(self.model.transformer_blocks):
            block_params = params["transformer_blocks"][i]
            block.ln1.scale = block_params["ln1"]["scale"]
            block.ln1.bias = block_params["ln1"]["bias"]
            block.attn.wq.w = block_params["attn"]["wq"]["w"]
            block.attn.wq.b = block_params["attn"]["wq"]["b"]
            block.attn.wk.w = block_params["attn"]["wk"]["w"]
            block.attn.wk.b = block_params["attn"]["wk"]["b"]
            block.attn.wv.w = block_params["attn"]["wv"]["w"]
            block.attn.wv.b = block_params["attn"]["wv"]["b"]
            block.attn.wo.w = block_params["attn"]["wo"]["w"]
            block.attn.wo.b = block_params["attn"]["wo"]["b"]
            block.ln2.scale = block_params["ln2"]["scale"]
            block.ln2.bias = block_params["ln2"]["bias"]
            block.ffn_dense1.w = block_params["ffn_dense1"]["w"]
            block.ffn_dense1.b = block_params["ffn_dense1"]["b"]
            block.ffn_dense2.w = block_params["ffn_dense2"]["w"]
            block.ffn_dense2.b = block_params["ffn_dense2"]["b"]
        self.model.output_proj.w = params["output_proj"]["w"]
        self.model.output_proj.b = params["output_proj"]["b"]

    def _sigma(self, t: jnp.ndarray) -> jnp.ndarray:
        """
        Compute the noise scale sigma(t) for VESDE.

        Args:
            t: jnp.ndarray of shape (batch_size, ) with diffusion times in [1e-5, 1.0].

        Returns:
            jnp.ndarray: sigma_t of shape (batch_size, ).
        """
        ratio = self.sigma_max / self.sigma_min
        sigma_t = self.sigma_min * (ratio ** t)
        return sigma_t

    def _sample_batch(self, batch_idx: int) -> dict:
        """
        Samples a mini-batch from the dataset and constructs input tokens and condition mask.

        Args:
            batch_idx (int): Index of the current mini-batch.

        Returns:
            dict: Dictionary with keys:
                  "x0": jnp.ndarray of shape (batch_size, seq_len, raw_value_dim) representing clean data.
                  "cond_mask": jnp.ndarray of shape (batch_size, seq_len) representing condition mask.
        """
        start = batch_idx * self.batch_size
        end = start + self.batch_size
        if end > self.n_samples:
            start = 0
            end = self.batch_size
        batch_parameters = self.data["parameters"][start:end]
        batch_observations = self.data["observations"][start:end]
        # Concatenate parameters and observations along token dimension.
        x0 = np.concatenate([batch_parameters, batch_observations], axis=1)
        # Convert to jnp.array and add an extra dimension for raw_value_dim (assume scalar tokens).
        x0 = jnp.array(x0)[..., None]  # shape: (batch_size, seq_len, 1)
        # Sample a condition mask.
        mask_types = self.config.get("simulation", {}).get("condition_mask", {}).get(
            "types", ["joint", "posterior", "likelihood", "random"]
        )
        self.rng, key = random.split(self.rng)
        mask_type_idx = random.randint(key, (), 0, len(mask_types))
        mask_type = mask_types[int(mask_type_idx)]
        total_tokens = x0.shape[1]
        if mask_type == "joint":
            cond_mask = jnp.zeros((self.batch_size, total_tokens), dtype=jnp.int32)
        elif mask_type == "posterior":
            d_params = batch_parameters.shape[1]
            d_obs = batch_observations.shape[1]
            cond_mask = jnp.concatenate([
                jnp.zeros((self.batch_size, d_params), dtype=jnp.int32),
                jnp.ones((self.batch_size, d_obs), dtype=jnp.int32)
            ], axis=1)
        elif mask_type == "likelihood":
            d_params = batch_parameters.shape[1]
            d_obs = batch_observations.shape[1]
            cond_mask = jnp.concatenate([
                jnp.ones((self.batch_size, d_params), dtype=jnp.int32),
                jnp.zeros((self.batch_size, d_obs), dtype=jnp.int32)
            ], axis=1)
        elif mask_type == "random":
            prob = self.config.get("simulation", {}).get("condition_mask", {}).get("random_mask_probability", 0.3)
            self.rng, key = random.split(self.rng)
            cond_mask = random.bernoulli(key, p=prob, shape=(self.batch_size, total_tokens)).astype(jnp.int32)
        else:
            cond_mask = jnp.zeros((self.batch_size, total_tokens), dtype=jnp.int32)
        return {"x0": x0, "cond_mask": cond_mask}

    def _loss_fn(self, params: dict, x0: jnp.ndarray, noise: jnp.ndarray, t: jnp.ndarray, cond_mask: jnp.ndarray) -> jnp.ndarray:
        """
        Denoising score matching loss function.

        Args:
            params (dict): Nested dictionary of model parameters.
            x0: Clean data tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_len, raw_value_dim).
            noise: Random noise tensor of the same shape as x0.
            t: Diffusion time tensor of shape (batch_size,).
            cond_mask: Condition mask tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_len).

        Returns:
            jnp.ndarray: Scalar loss value.
        """
        # Update the model with the provided parameters.
        self.set_params(params)
        batch_size, seq_len, raw_dim = x0.shape
        sigma_t = self._sigma(t)  # shape: (batch_size,)
        sigma_t_exp = sigma_t.reshape(batch_size, 1, 1)  # reshape for broadcasting
        # Forward SDE: compute noised data x_t = x0 + sigma_t * noise.
        x_t = x0 + sigma_t_exp * noise
        # Enforce condition: observed tokens remain equal to x0.
        cond_mask_exp = jnp.expand_dims(cond_mask, axis=-1)
        x_MC_t = cond_mask_exp * x0 + (1 - cond_mask_exp) * x_t
        # Prepare mask dictionary for the model.
        mask = {"cond": cond_mask}
        # Model prediction: compute predicted score for x_MC_t.
        predicted_score = self.model.compute_score(x_MC_t, t, mask)
        # Ground-truth score: -(x_t - x0) / (sigma(t)^2).
        sigma_t_sq = sigma_t_exp ** 2
        target_score = -(x_t - x0) / sigma_t_sq
        # Compute loss over unobserved components only.
        weight = 1 - cond_mask_exp
        loss = jnp.mean((weight * (predicted_score - target_score)) ** 2)
        return loss

    def _update_step(self, params: dict, opt_state, x0: jnp.ndarray, noise: jnp.ndarray, t: jnp.ndarray, cond_mask: jnp.ndarray):
        """
        Performs one update step: computes the loss, its gradients,
        and applies parameter updates.

        Args:
            params (dict): Current model parameters.
            opt_state: Optimizer state.
            x0: Clean data tensor.
            noise: Random noise tensor.
            t: Diffusion time tensor.
            cond_mask: Condition mask tensor.

        Returns:
            Tuple containing:
                - loss_value: Scalar loss.
                - new_params: Updated parameters dictionary.
                - new_opt_state: New optimizer state.
        """
        loss_value, grads = jax.value_and_grad(self._loss_fn)(params, x0, noise, t, cond_mask)
        updates, opt_state = self.optimizer.update(grads, opt_state, params)
        new_params = optax.apply_updates(params, updates)
        return loss_value, new_params, opt_state

    def train(self) -> None:
        """
        Runs the training loop over epochs and batches.
        Implements mini-batch sampling, noise injection, loss computation,
        gradient calculation, and parameter updates.
        """
        num_batches = self.n_samples // self.batch_size
        best_loss = float("inf")
        patience = 5  # fixed patience for early stopping (can be configured)
        patience_counter = 0

        for epoch in range(self.max_epochs):
            epoch_loss = 0.0
            for batch_idx in range(num_batches):
                # Sample a mini-batch.
                batch = self._sample_batch(batch_idx)
                x0 = batch["x0"]  # shape: (batch_size, seq_len, 1)
                cond_mask = batch["cond_mask"]  # shape: (batch_size, seq_len)
                # Sample noise and diffusion times.
                self.rng, noise_key, t_key = random.split(self.rng, 3)
                noise = random.normal(noise_key, shape=x0.shape)
                t = random.uniform(t_key, shape=(self.batch_size,), minval=1e-5, maxval=1.0)
                # Compute one optimization update.
                loss_value, self.params, self.opt_state = self._update_step(
                    self.params, self.opt_state, x0, noise, t, cond_mask
                )
                epoch_loss += loss_value
            avg_epoch_loss = epoch_loss / num_batches
            print(f"Epoch {epoch + 1}, Average Loss: {avg_epoch_loss}")

            # Early stopping based on validation loss improvement.
            if avg_epoch_loss < best_loss:
                best_loss = avg_epoch_loss
                patience_counter = 0
            else:
                patience_counter += 1
            if self.early_stopping and patience_counter >= patience:
                print("Early stopping triggered.")
                break

        # After training, update the model with final parameters.
        self.set_params(self.params)
        print("Training complete.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # For standalone testing of the Trainer.
    import yaml
    from dataset_loader import DatasetLoader
    from model import Model

    try:
        with open("config.yaml", "r") as f:
            config = yaml.safe_load(f)
    except Exception as e:
        print("Error loading configuration:", e)
        config = {}

    # Instantiate dataset loader and load simulated data.
    loader = DatasetLoader(config)
    # Use the "gaussian_linear" simulation task for testing.
    data = loader.load_data(task="gaussian_linear")
    # Instantiate the model.
    model = Model(config)
    # Instantiate and run the trainer.
    trainer = Trainer(model, data, config)
    start_time = time.time()
    trainer.train()
    end_time = time.time()
    print(f"Training time: {end_time - start_time:.2f} seconds")
